electric_chapter_intro.summary:
### Tier 4 is the "site becomes a power system"

Steel/DC got you: heavy plant, dynamos, local DC networks, and motors.
Now you add what makes power go *far* and *scale*:

- AC generation and switching practice
- transformers (step up / step down)
- three-phase transmission for distance + motors
- rectification back to DC for old loads and heavy processes
- early electronics (vacuum tubes) as the bridge into instrumentation + control
- plant-wide meters, relays, alarms
- early regulation and automatic control
- a central control room that makes the plant readable
electric_chapter_intro.loreShort: This is the jump from 'machines' to 'a coordinated power-and-control site'.
electric_chapter_intro.notes: Gate on steel_IV_complete (steel chapter checkpoint); auto-complete when opened.

electric_ac_experiment.summary:
### Build a small AC test bay

Goal: a controlled place where you can safely energize circuits, switch loads,
and prove you can run lamps without "mystery wiring".

This is where AC becomes a tool instead of a theory.
electric_ac_experiment.loreShort: AC wins when you can transform voltage and distribute power safely.
electric_ac_experiment.notes: Use this as a teaching bay: isolation, switching, fusing, visible load effects.

tasks.desc: Have or place a small AC generator/alternator for test power.
Have or place a basic switchboard panel (mounting + isolation point).
Stock heavy knife switches for isolation points.
Bring lamps online (fixtures + carbon-filament lamps).
Craft the AC generator set (optional if obtained another way).

electric_transformers_yard.summary:
### Build a transformer yard

Place step-up near generation and step-down near loads.
Run a short "high voltage" span between them using proper insulators.

This teaches the real AC advantage: distance without absurd copper loss.
electric_transformers_yard.loreShort: Transformers let you trade current for voltage so transmission losses drop.
electric_transformers_yard.notes: Make placement matter: generator house vs load bay vs substation.

tasks.desc: Have or place a step-up transformer near generation, and a step-down near loads.
Stock insulators, insulated wire, and copper bus bars for a safe yard build.
Craft the transformer sets (optional if obtained another way).

electric_switchgear_protection.summary:
### Switchgear and protection discipline

Before you push power farther, you need safe isolation and protection:
switches, fuses, bus bars, and insulating hardware.
electric_switchgear_protection.loreShort: Early switchgear is open and manual, but protection discipline is non-negotiable.
electric_switchgear_protection.notes: Teach safe isolation first: switches + fuses + clear mounting before bigger feeders.

tasks.desc: Have or place switchboard panels for mounting protection hardware.
Stock heavy knife switches and rewireable fuses for isolation and protection.
Stock copper bus bars and insulators for safe mounting and clearance.

electric_three_phase_grid.summary:
### Upgrade to three-phase

Three-phase is the serious industrial standard:
smooth rotating fields for motors, and copper-efficient transmission.

Bring up a three-phase alternator and a district AC network segment.
electric_three_phase_grid.loreShort: Three-phase becomes the default for transmission + heavy motors because it runs smooth and scales well.
electric_three_phase_grid.notes: Visually differentiate 3-phase lines/gear; make the grid feel 'bigger' than the test bay.

tasks.desc: Have or place a three-phase generator/alternator.
Have or place a district AC network segment (substation/network anchor).
Stock the extra insulators and insulated wire required for multi-phase distribution.

electric_ac_motors_floor.summary:
### Put motors where the work is

Replace some shaft-only dependency with local induction motors.
Not everything becomes electric overnight -- but critical tools stop being hostage to long line shafts.
electric_ac_motors_floor.loreShort: Induction motors become factory workhorses because they're rugged and easy to wire.
electric_ac_motors_floor.notes: Encourage a mixed floor: some shafts remain, some stations become motor-driven islands.

tasks.desc: Have or place induction motors for key tools/hoists.
Keep some mechanical distribution parts on hand (transition is real: shafts + belts don't instantly vanish).
Have or place an electric-capable machine shop bay.

electric_instruments_and_relays.summary:
### Make the plant readable and protected

Install meters, relays, and alarms so operators can *see load* and *get warned*
before failures cascade.

Target at least:
- one boiler house feed
- one generator feeder
- one outgoing line/load bay
electric_instruments_and_relays.loreShort: Central panels and relays replace 'walk over and listen' as plants get bigger and more dangerous.
electric_instruments_and_relays.notes: This should feel like safety discipline: measurement, isolation, and alarm logic.

tasks.desc: Stock panel meters: ammeters and voltmeters.
Stock relays and alarms for basic protection and operator warning.
Stock protection + isolation hardware: switches and fuses.

electric_rectifier_bay.summary:
### Feed DC loads from the AC plant

You already own DC infrastructure from Tier 3.
Now you build the bridge: a rectifier bay that takes incoming AC and produces heavy DC.

This is historically honest: AC doesn't delete DC -- it *feeds and extends it*.
electric_rectifier_bay.loreShort: Big rectifiers converted AC into traction/plating DC long before solid state.
electric_rectifier_bay.notes: Treat this as a loud, hot, maintenance-heavy bay with clear protection/isolation points.

tasks.desc: Have or place a mercury-arc rectifier set (or equivalent heavy rectifier).
Stock copper bus bars, switches, and fuses for a protected DC output bay.

electric_vacuum_tube_lab.summary:
### Build the first electronics capability

Set up a glass + vacuum bench and produce simple diodes and triodes.
You're not building a full radio stack here -- you're proving:
- one-way conduction (diode)
- controlled gain/switching behavior (triode)

This becomes a bridge into sensing, amplification, and control.
electric_vacuum_tube_lab.loreShort: Thermionic valves launch electronics by turning hot cathodes and vacuum into rectification and amplification.
electric_vacuum_tube_lab.notes: Keep it tangible: vacuum quality, filament supply, and fragile glass output.

tasks.desc: Have or place a glass bench and lab vacuum pump (the minimum tube-making setup).
Have a filament supply (low-voltage DC) for tube heating and test circuits.
Produce a starter batch of diodes and triodes.

electric_regulators_control.summary:
### Regulators and closed-loop control

Bring in early automatic regulation (pneumatic/analog controllers):
- sensors read process value
- regulators compare to a setpoint
- control valves move steam or feedwater

This is the shift from "watching gauges" to supervising systems that hold steady on their own.
electric_regulators_control.loreShort: Regulators and pneumatic controllers keep pressure and level stable before modern digital control.
electric_regulators_control.notes: Keep the loop readable: setpoint, process value, and valve position should be visible during tuning.

tasks.desc: Have regulators/controllers available (pneumatic or analog control class).
Stock sensors for pressure and level measurement.
Stock control valves so the controller can actually move the process.

electric_control_room.summary:
### Centralize supervision

Build a control room wall:
- mimic diagram showing boilers, generators, transformers, feeders, and key loops
- meters and alarms that pull plant state into one place

This is the shift from "walking the site" to "running the site".
electric_control_room.loreShort: Control rooms evolve because big plants can't be run by physically standing next to every machine.
electric_control_room.notes: Make alarms meaningful: trips, overload warnings, low-level alerts, etc.

tasks.desc: Have or place a mimic panel for the AC grid (central visualization).
Have or place switchboard panels for the control room wall.
Stock meters and alarms for the control room wall.

electric_checkpoint.summary:
### Tier 4 online

Confirm the full integration:

- three-phase AC grid and distribution
- induction motors on the floor
- rectifier bay feeding heavy DC loads
- vacuum tube electronics capability
- plant-wide instrumentation and protection
- regulators and automatic control
- a control room that makes the whole site readable

Next tier can go solid-state (diodes/transistors), electronic control, and then digital.
electric_checkpoint.loreShort: This is the mature heavy-industrial 'power + control' site before solid-state and computation take over.
electric_checkpoint.notes: Hard gate into the next tier (solid-state + digital control).

electric_chem_gateway.summary:
### Electrolysis opens the chemical frontier

Your rectifier bay now outputs heavy DC. The next step: using that current
to split matter into things that don't occur naturally in pure form.

- **Chlor-alkali cell** (DiAPHRAGM or membrane): NaCl brine + DC → Cl2 + NaOH + H2
- **Downs cell** (fused-salt): molten NaCl + DC → Na metal + Cl2
- **Müller-Kühne HF**: CaF2 (fluorite) + H2SO4 → HF + CaSO4

These are the three master reagents for all Tier 4+ chemistry:
Cl2 for chlorination, NaOH for leaching/digestion, HF for niobium/zirconium/fluorite processing.
electric_chem_gateway.loreShort: Industrial electrolysis turns salt water into chlorine, caustic soda and hydrogen -- feedstocks for almost everything that follows.
electric_chem_gateway.notes: Bridge from the power chapter to industrial chemistry. Requires arc_furnace_electric for the Downs cell.

tasks.desc: Process brine solution through the chlor-alkali cell.
Collect chlorine gas and sodium hydroxide (caustic soda) from the cell outputs.
Produce hydrofluoric acid from fluorite + sulfuric acid.
Produce sodium metal via the Downs cell (fused-salt electrolysis of NaCl).

electric_arc_furnace_chem.summary:
### Electric arc furnace chemistry chain

The electric arc furnace does more than make steel: its extreme temperatures
drive carbothermic and thermite reactions conventional furnaces cannot reach.

- **MG-Si** (98% silicon): SiO2 + 2C → Si + 2CO at ~2000°C -- gateway to semiconductor silicon
- **Ferrochrome**: chromite + coke → FeCr -- essential for stainless and superalloy grades
- **Ferronickel**: pentlandite + coke + limestone → FeNi -- nickel source for stainless, Inconel
- **Stainless steel 18-8**: Fe + FeCr + FeNi in argon-oxygen decarburization converter
electric_arc_furnace_chem.loreShort: The electric arc furnace's precise high heat reduces metal oxides directly to alloying metals that conventional furnaces cannot produce.
electric_arc_furnace_chem.notes: Station: arc_furnace_electric. MG-Si output feeds the trichlorosilane / semiconductor chain in Solid-State tier.

tasks.desc: Produce metallurgical-grade silicon (98% pure) by carbothermic reduction of silica.
Smelt ferrochrome (from chromite + coke) and ferronickel (from pentlandite + coke).
Smelt a stainless steel 18-8 ingot from the ferroalloys in an AOD converter.

electric_aluminum_smelting.summary:
### Smelt aluminium from bauxite

Aluminium is the most abundant structural metal on Earth but
exists only as oxides locked in bauxite. Two electrochemical steps liberate it:

1. **Bayer process**: NaOH dissolves bauxite → aluminium hydroxide → filter → calcine → Al2O3
2. **Hall-Heroult cell**: Al2O3 dissolved in molten cryolite + DC + carbon anodes → liquid Al + CO2

Result: one of the lightest, most corrosion-proof structural metals.
Essential for aircraft, vehicles, overhead power cables, and all future rocketry.
electric_aluminum_smelting.loreShort: Before the Hall-Heroult process (1886) aluminium was rarer than gold, sold for more than platinum, and used for Napoleon III's finest cutlery.
electric_aluminum_smelting.notes: Requires carbon_anode (Acheson coke + coal tar pitch) and cryolite (synthetic, from fluorite + HF + NaOH).

tasks.desc: Have cryolite bath salt and baked carbon anodes ready for the reduction pot.
Produce aluminium ingots via Hall-Heroult electrolysis.
Roll sheet and produce an aerospace-grade Al alloy for structures.

electric_refined_metals.summary:
### Purify metals to industry-grade purity

**Copper electrorefining**: blister copper (~98.5%) cast as anodes, dissolved in
CuSO4/H2SO4, and re-deposited onto stainless cathodes → 99.99% Cu.
Precious metals (Ag, Au, Se) fall as valuable anode slime.

**Nickel electrorefining (Sherritt Gordon process)**: pentlandite concentrate
→ ammoniacal leach → purification → electrodeposition → 99.9% Ni cathode.

Nickel cathode is the input for all downstream superalloy VIM melts: stainless, Inconel.
electric_refined_metals.loreShort: Electrorefining uses the same DC that drives motors to deposit ultra-pure metal atom by atom from an impure anode into a pure cathode.
electric_refined_metals.notes: Station: arc_furnace_electric + machine_shop_electric. Copper cathode and nickel cathode feed all higher-tier alloy chains.

tasks.desc: Provide blister copper anodes for the electrorefining bath.
Process nickel concentrate through the Sherritt Gordon leach-electro route.
Roll stainless steel sheet for fabrication stock.

electric_token_convert.title: Trade In: Industry IV → Tech V
electric_token_convert.summary: Bridge the industrial era to the tech era. Exchange 5 Tier IV industry tokens for 1 Tier V tech token. Repeatable.

metal_chapter_intro.summary:
### The Metal Ages

You have fire, clay, and a kiln that reaches over 1,000 degrees.
You have everything you need to work metal.

Three metals. Three ages. Each one harder to make and more useful than the last.

- **Copper**: hammer it cold, then learn to melt it
- **Bronze**: find tin, smelt it, alloy it with copper
- **Iron**: gather bog iron, build a bloomery, hammer out wrought iron

The Steam chapter will ask you for a lot of iron.
This is where you learn to make it.

[hint]**Use the recipe browser throughout this chapter.**  Quest tasks show *what* to produce but not the exact ingredients.  Hover any item and press **R** to see its recipe, or press **U** to see what uses it.  Every process in this chapter is discoverable that way -- you don't need to guess.[/hint]

[hint]**Both paths reward you equally.** You must complete the Bronze path (bronze pickaxe) AND the Iron path (iron hammer) to reach the checkpoint.  Both tools unlock Tier 2 ore mining -- coal, hematite, cassiterite in harder deposits.  You will need them for the Steam chapter.[/hint]
metal_chapter_intro.loreShort: Copper, bronze, and iron defined successive ages of human technology, each requiring new techniques to extract and work metal.
metal_chapter_intro.lore:
### What changes here

You stop finding materials and start producing them.
That distinction -- found vs. produced -- is the conceptual leap behind all of metallurgy.
metal_chapter_intro.notes: Gated by stone_II_complete; auto-completes on open.

copper_cold_work.summary:
### Cold-hammered copper

Native copper doesn't need a furnace. It needs a hammerstone and patience.

Cold hammering work-hardens copper -- each strike makes it stronger,
but eventually it becomes brittle. Anneal it in your campfire to
restore workability, then hammer again.

This is the Copper Age. It predates smelting by thousands of years.
copper_cold_work.loreShort: Cold-working native copper was humanity's first use of metal, thousands of years before anyone learned to smelt ore.
copper_cold_work.lore:
### Annealing

Heating metal and letting it cool slowly was discovered before smelting.
Copperworkers noticed that fire softened their work-hardened pieces.
This observation -- that heat changes metal -- is the conceptual leap
that eventually leads to all of metallurgy.

tasks.desc: Mine native copper (already visible -- no survey needed).
Hammer native copper into workable nuggets at a stone anvil.

copper_crucible_smelt.summary:
### Crucible smelting

Your kiln can reach 1,100 °C. Copper melts at 1,085 °C.

You already have everything you need.

Build a clay crucible. Load it with native copper. Fire it in the kiln.
Pour the liquid copper into a stone mold.
copper_crucible_smelt.loreShort: Crucible smelting -- melting metal in a small clay vessel inside a kiln -- was the first controlled liquid metal process.
copper_crucible_smelt.lore:
### This is the moment your game changes

You can now produce a material that is *liquid* and can be *shaped*.
From here, every metal follows the same logic: heat, reduce, cast.
The only variables are temperature requirements and reduction chemistry.

tasks.desc: Craft clay crucibles for holding a metal charge.
Carve a stone ingot mold.
Smelt native copper in the crucible and cast copper ingots.

copper_tools.summary:
### Copper tools

Cast copper is soft but workable. Shape it into simple tools --
awls, chisels, small blades.

Copper tools wear quickly compared to stone, but they can be
re-cast when they break. Stone can't.

That's the real advantage: repairability.
copper_tools.loreShort: Copper tools were softer than good stone but could be recast indefinitely, making metal the first truly recyclable tool material.
copper_tools.lore:
### Why copper matters

Copper is not better than stone for cutting.
It's better than stone for *remaking*.

A broken flint blade is waste. A broken copper awl goes back in the crucible.

tasks.desc: Stockpile copper ingots for toolmaking and alloying.

cassiterite_survey.summary:
### Find the tin

Bronze isn't copper. It's copper + tin.

Tin comes from cassiterite -- a heavy, dark pebble found in stream gravels.
It looks like nothing. It changes everything.

Look for dark, dense pebbles in stream deposits.
Cassiterite is visible to the naked eye if you know what you're looking for.
cassiterite_survey.loreShort: Cassiterite (tin oxide) was historically found as stream-bed placer deposits -- heavy dark pebbles that early metalworkers learned to recognize and collect.
cassiterite_survey.lore:
### Why tin matters

Tin occurs in relatively few places, unlike common copper.
Bronze Age "globalization" happened because of this single alloy requirement.
Trade routes formed specifically to supply tin to copper-producing regions.

tasks.desc: Collect cassiterite ore from stream gravels.

tin_smelt.summary:
### Smelt tin

Tin melts at 232 °C -- well within campfire range, far below your kiln's capacity.

Load cassiterite into a crucible with charcoal. The carbon reduces the tin oxide
to metallic tin. Pour it into your stone mold.

Tin by itself is too soft for tools. But mixed with copper...
tin_smelt.loreShort: Tin's low melting point (232 °C) meant it could be smelted in simple fires long before iron or even copper smelting was mastered.
tin_smelt.lore:
### The ratio

Bronze is approximately 90% copper, 10% tin.
Too much tin makes it brittle. Too little and you haven't improved on pure copper.

tasks.desc: Smelt cassiterite into tin ingots in the crucible.

bronze_casting.summary:
### Cast bronze

Melt copper and tin together in a crucible.
The ratio matters: roughly 5 copper to 1 tin for gameplay.

Pour into molds. Cool. You have bronze.

Bronze is harder than copper, holds an edge better, and casts more precisely.
It's the first *engineered* material -- an alloy designed for performance.
bronze_casting.loreShort: Bronze was so superior to copper that it reoriented entire economies around securing tin supply.
bronze_casting.lore:
### What bronze unlocks

The bronze pickaxe unlocks Tier 2 ore mining --
hematite, magnetite, and sulfide ores that stone and copper tools can't touch.

You now have a reason to go deeper.

tasks.desc: Alloy copper and tin to produce bronze ingots.
Forge a bronze pickaxe (unlocks Tier 2 mining).

bog_iron_survey.summary:
### Bog iron

Bog iron forms in wetland areas where iron-bearing groundwater meets organic acids.

It's impure, spongy, and weak compared to later iron.
But it's everywhere. A village smith can collect it without mining equipment.

Look for orange-brown precipitate at the margins of standing water.
Bog iron is auto-surveyed -- you can see it without a geology hammer.
bog_iron_survey.loreShort: Bog iron democratized metal technology -- any community with a wetland and charcoal supply could produce iron without long-distance trade.
bog_iron_survey.lore:
### Why iron matters

Iron broke the palace monopolies on metallurgy that bronze had created.
Before bog iron, having metal tools required either living near a mine
or being part of a trade network.
After bog iron, any community with a wetland could make iron.

tasks.desc: Gather bog iron from wetland deposits.

roasting_pit_build.summary:
### Roasting pit

Raw bog iron is full of moisture and volatile impurities.
Roasting drives these off before the bloomery step.

Build a simple roasting pit -- a shallow fire bed with ore layered on top.
The heat doesn't melt anything. It just dries and pre-treats the ore
so the bloomery can reduce it more efficiently.
roasting_pit_build.loreShort: Roasting ore before smelting was an early optimization that improved bloomery yields by removing moisture and some impurities.
roasting_pit_build.lore:
### Not optional

Without roasting, your bloomery will consume more charcoal
and produce a smaller, slag-heavy bloom.
Roasting is where "I found some ore" becomes "I prepared a charge."

tasks.desc: Build and place a roasting pit.
Roast bog iron to prepare it for the bloomery.

clay_bloomery_build.summary:
### Build a bloomery

A bloomery is a shaft furnace -- not a kiln.

The key difference: you force air through the base using bellows.
This gets the temperature high enough to reduce iron oxide to metallic iron,
even though it's not hot enough to melt iron.

Build the shaft from clay and stone. Add a tuyere (air inlet).
Connect bellows. Prepare charcoal.
clay_bloomery_build.loreShort: Bloomeries were the first iron-producing furnaces -- shaft structures where forced air and charcoal reduced ore to a spongy iron mass.
clay_bloomery_build.lore:
### What the bloomery does

Carbon monoxide from burning charcoal strips oxygen from the iron ore.
The iron collects as a spongy mass called a bloom.
It never melts. It just... consolidates.

tasks.desc: Build and place a clay bloomery furnace.
Have bellows ready for forced air.
Stock charcoal for the bloomery charge.

bloom_extraction.summary:
### Extract the bloom

Charge the bloomery: roasted ore and charcoal in alternating layers.
Work the bellows. The reduction reaction takes time.

The result is a spongy mass of iron and slag -- a bloom.
It is NOT liquid iron. It never melts. It just consolidates.

Pull the bloom out while it's still red-hot. Work fast.
bloom_extraction.loreShort: The bloom is the raw product of a bloomery -- a hot, spongy mass of iron and trapped slag that must be hammered to become usable metal.
bloom_extraction.lore:
### The bloom isn't finished iron

It's iron contaminated with slag. The hammering is what removes the slag
and aligns the iron grain structure. Without hammering, the bloom is useless.

tasks.desc: Run the bloomery and extract iron blooms.

forge_wrought_iron.summary:
### Forge wrought iron

The bloom needs to be worked.

Reheat it to orange-red. Strike it with a heavy hammer.
The slag squirts out as sparks and scale. Fold it. Reheat. Repeat.

This process is called consolidation. What you're making is wrought iron --
the same material every pre-industrial metalwork is built from.
forge_wrought_iron.loreShort: Wrought iron is produced by repeatedly heating and hammering a bloom to expel slag -- a labor-intensive process that produces tough, malleable iron.
forge_wrought_iron.lore:
### The payoff

The iron hammer unlocks Tier 2 mining alongside the bronze pickaxe.
Both the Bronze and Iron paths open the same deeper ores --
historically accurate, and it gives you two routes to the Steam chapter.

tasks.desc: Build and place a forge hearth.
Hammer blooms into wrought iron bars at the forge.
Forge an iron hammer head and haft it.

metal_ages_checkpoint.summary:
### The Metal Ages complete

**Both paths required. Bronze AND iron. Check your quest map -- one of them isn't done.**

You now understand metal.

Not as a found object -- as a produced material.

Copper from native deposits. Bronze from two ores combined in a specific ratio.
Iron from a reduction furnace that took all day to run.
Each one is harder to make and more useful than the last.

The Steam chapter is going to ask you for a lot of iron.
You know how to make it.
metal_ages_checkpoint.loreShort: From cold-hammered copper through bronze alloying to bloomery iron, you have traced the full arc of ancient metallurgy.
metal_ages_checkpoint.lore:
### What you've learned

- Metal can be shaped by hammering, melting, or both
- Alloying (bronze) creates materials better than either component
- Reduction (bloomery) extracts metal from ore using carbon chemistry
- Every metal process is: ore + heat + chemistry = metal + waste

The Steam tier builds on all of this.

ore_guide_intro.summary: Found something you don't recognise? Pick a category on the left and match what you see. Every entry has an 'In Conduvia' section describing exactly what it looks like in-game.
ore_guide_intro.loreShort: Real deposits have repeatable tells: colour, staining, host rock, scale, and landscape context. This guide is organised around what YOU see, not geology textbooks.
ore_guide_intro.notes:
STEP 1 -- PICK A CATEGORY
• White crust / flat dry ground → Salt Flat & Crystal Deposits
• Brassy, metallic, or rusty-stained rock → Metal Ore Veins
• Red or gray banded cliffs / massive gray rock → Iron Ores & Bulk Rock
• River pebbles / bog nodules / volcanic glass → Surface & River Finds
• Nothing obvious, scanner pinging → Deep & Instrument Finds

STEP 2 -- READ THE ENTRY
• VISUAL APPEARANCE -- what it looks like up close.
• LANDMARK / RECOGNITION -- what the terrain around it looks like.
• IN CONDUVIA -- exactly what to expect in-game.

STEP 3 -- CONFIRM WITH SCANNER
• When visual cues are ambiguous the scanner gives you the direct ID.
• Some late-game ores have no reliable visual tell at all -- instruments first.

ore_hub_evaporite.summary: White crusts, salt pans, and layered crystal beds in dry basins. Halite, gypsum, sylvite.
ore_hub_evaporite.loreShort: These minerals form where water evaporates faster than it's replenished, leaving dissolved salts behind as solid layers.

ore_hub_sulfide.summary: Brassy, metallic, or dark minerals in quartz veins. Green staining = copper nearby. Rusty cap = look below. Chalcopyrite, galena, sphalerite, pyrite.
ore_hub_sulfide.loreShort: Hot fluids move metals through cracks and deposit them in veins. The surface shows staining and gossans; the metal hides a metre or two down.

ore_hub_oxide_industrial.summary: The big feedstocks: iron for steel, limestone for cement, silica for glass, bauxite for aluminium. Usually massive, not small veins. Hematite, magnetite, bauxite, limestone, silica.
ore_hub_oxide_industrial.loreShort: These form in enormous sedimentary layers or deep weathering profiles. The scale itself is a recognition cue -- they're hard to miss once you know what you're standing on.

ore_hub_primitive.summary: Materials you can grab with no mining at all: riverbed pebbles, bog nodules, surface float. Flint, obsidian, native copper, bog iron, cassiterite.
ore_hub_primitive.loreShort: These are the first things a new player can collect. No vein required -- walk, look, and pick up.

ore_hub_hightech.summary: Late-game materials you won't identify by eye alone. Use your scanner. Spodumene (lithium), rare earth ore, uraninite.
ore_hub_hightech.loreShort: Visual cues exist but are subtle. By the time you need these, you have the instruments to find them.

tasks.desc: Obtain 12 raw halite ore

ore_halite.summary: Food seasoning; chemical feedstock (chlorine/caustic soda); de-icing.
ore_halite.loreShort: Evaporite deposits in arid basins -- precipitates from drying seawater or saline lakes; massive bedded layers or salt domes.
ore_halite.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Clear to white cubic crystals; can be reddish or orange from mineral impurities.
• Soft; breaks into clean cube cleavage fragments.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Huge stratified beds hundreds of meters thick; salt domes can be kilometers across.
• Often mined underground via room-and-pillar methods, or produced via evaporation ponds.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• White crusts on dry lakebeds; polygon-cracked pans; little to no vegetation.
• Outcrops are rare in wet climates because salt dissolves readily -- an arid basin with white crust is the primary cue.

IN CONDUVIA
• Expect bright-white flats and subtle cube-crystal clusters in desert terrain.
• Cracked salt-pan plates; ground may behave differently underfoot.

tasks.desc: Obtain 12 raw gypsum ore

ore_gypsum.summary: Plaster and drywall; cement additive; soil conditioner.
ore_gypsum.loreShort: Evaporite layers interbedded with limestone, shale, and halite; forms during evaporation or hydration of anhydrite; also from saline groundwater and hot springs.
ore_gypsum.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• White or gray chalky massive rock; can be clear (selenite) or fibrous (satin spar).
• Very soft -- scratches with a fingernail; can form bladed crystals or desert rose clusters.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Thick beds meters to tens of meters; common open-pit quarries.
• Crystals can be exceptionally large in rare pockets -- meter scale in some localities.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• White slopes and cliffs; crumbly scratchable outcrop; powdery crust soils; sparse vegetation from soil salinity.

IN CONDUVIA
• Light gray and white seams and layered bands in sedimentary terrain.
• Rare selenite pockets expose translucent blade-like crystal clusters.

tasks.desc: Obtain 12 raw sylvite ore

ore_sylvite.summary: Fertilizer potassium source; potassium chemical feedstock.
ore_sylvite.loreShort: Late-stage evaporite in closed basins -- forms after extreme evaporation; interlayered with halite and anhydrite in ancient evaporite sequences.
ore_sylvite.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Colorless to pale orange or red cubic crystals; often pinkish with mineral impurities.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Localized beds and lenses (meters) within thick salt sequences; mined underground or via solution methods.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Rarely exposed at surface; occurs in association with other evaporite minerals deeper in arid basin terrain.

IN CONDUVIA
• Deep in salt-bearing terrain, look for rare pinkish cube-crystal clusters nested within halite layers.

ore_chalcopyrite.summary: Copper ore -- primary source of copper for wiring, electronics, and alloys.
ore_chalcopyrite.loreShort: Hydrothermal veins and disseminations in igneous intrusions; common in porphyry copper systems and massive sulfide settings.
ore_chalcopyrite.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Brassy yellow metallic mineral; tarnishes iridescent purple, blue, or green.
• Softer than pyrite; often massive and grainy with rare well-formed crystals.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Veins: cm to m thick. Porphyry systems: huge low-grade volumes (km-scale pit type).
• Often mined as large bulk-rock operations when found in porphyry style.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Green malachite and blue azurite stains in oxidized zones.
• Rusty gossan caps can indicate sulfides below; quartz veins with brassy specks are a reliable surface tell.

IN CONDUVIA
• Yellow-gold metallic chunks embedded in quartz or stone veins, with green staining around them.
• Green staining near a vein is a reliable indicator of a copper system -- learn to read it.

ore_galena.summary: Lead ore (batteries, shielding); often carries significant silver value.
ore_galena.loreShort: Low to mid temperature hydrothermal veins, especially in limestone and dolostone (MVT style); often with sphalerite, fluorite, and calcite.
ore_galena.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Lead-gray metallic cubes with perfect cubic cleavage; very heavy for size.
• Soft; scratches easily; fresh faces are shiny, tarnished surfaces go dull.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Veins usually under 1 m thick but laterally extensive; replacement bodies can be tens of meters.
• Common underground mining geometry.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• White coatings in weathered zones (anglesite and cerussite).
• Often found in limestone caves and veins alongside calcite and fluorite.

IN CONDUVIA
• Heavy dark-gray cube chunks embedded in limestone veins.
• One of the densest ores you'll carry -- noticeably heavier than its size suggests.

ore_sphalerite.summary: Zinc ore -- galvanizing, alloys and brass, batteries; may carry minor cadmium and indium.
ore_sphalerite.loreShort: Common with galena in limestone-hosted deposits (MVT) and in VMS systems with copper minerals.
ore_sphalerite.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Brown to honey-yellow resinous crystals; iron-rich varieties are black.
• Translucent when pure; freshly broken faces can carry a faint sulfur smell.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Vein and replacement bodies similar to galena; the two minerals are often intimately mixed.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Oxidized zones may show pale green smithsonite coatings.
• Look for shiny brown-black chips in limestone vugs near galena cubes -- they commonly occur together.

IN CONDUVIA
• Often occurs in the same vein district as galena -- if you found one, check the surrounding area.
• Resinous brown to black crystal chunks with a distinct glossy surface.

ore_pyrite.summary: Not a modern iron ore; historically used for sulfur and sparks; a major indicator mineral for nearby metal systems.
ore_pyrite.loreShort: Extremely common sulfide in hydrothermal, igneous, metamorphic, and sedimentary settings; forms cubes, pyritohedra, and massive granular zones.
ore_pyrite.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Brassy yellow metallic cubes with characteristic striations; harder than chalcopyrite.
• Often forms nodules ("pyrite suns") or tiny glitter points disseminated in shale.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Scattered grains to massive ore bodies; veins can be meters thick.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Weathering creates rusty gossans and orange-stained drainage.
• In outcrop: tiny gold-cube glitter; in weathered zones: red-brown iron staining.

IN CONDUVIA
• Pyrite alone isn't the prize -- but where it appears, more valuable sulfides are often nearby.
• Rusty orange drainage stains and gossan caps are your environmental cue when prospecting.

ore_hematite.summary: Primary iron ore for steel; also a red pigment (ochre).
ore_hematite.loreShort: Commonly from banded iron formations (BIFs) and enrichment and weathering zones; often associated with magnetite and goethite.
ore_hematite.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Specular hematite: steel-gray or black shiny flakes.
• Earthy hematite: brick-red dull masses; diagnostic reddish-brown streak when scratched.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Can form enormous formation-scale deposits; open pits hundreds of meters deep.
• Also occurs as smaller ironstone beds or residual boulders.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Red soils; striped red and gray banding in BIF cliffs.
• Leaves rust-red powder when scratched -- a reliable hand test.

IN CONDUVIA
• Red-brown seams and banded layers on cliff faces; banded iron formations show alternating red and gray stripes.
• Mining raises red dust -- a visual tell distinct from other iron ores.

ore_magnetite.summary: Iron ore; also produces natural magnetism (the basis of historic lodestone compasses).
ore_magnetite.loreShort: Occurs in igneous and metamorphic rocks, layered intrusions, skarns, and iron formations; also concentrates as black sand placers.
ore_magnetite.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Black metallic to dull mineral; granular to octahedral crystals.
• Magnetic -- the single clearest field identification test.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Seams in intrusions (meters thick) or massive iron formation and taconite systems.
• Black sands can blanket beaches and river bars.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• A magnet sticks to it; black sand streaks on beaches and river bars are a surface indicator.
• Dense concentrations can interfere with compass readings.

IN CONDUVIA
• Black ore lumps or octahedral crystal clusters.
• A compass near a magnetite deposit will behave unreliably -- a useful prospecting cue.

ore_bauxite.summary: Primary aluminum ore (alumina to aluminum); can carry gallium as a byproduct.
ore_bauxite.loreShort: Tropical laterite weathering concentrate of aluminum hydroxides; forms blanket-like soil profiles over suitable parent rock.
ore_bauxite.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Red-brown earthy mass with pisolites (pea-sized pellets).
• Clay-like texture; mottled whites, greys, and browns; no large crystals.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• A few to tens of meters thick but spread over enormous plateaus; typically strip-mined.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Distinct red lateritic soils; soft and diggable; the pea-sized pisolite pellets are the diagnostic feature.

IN CONDUVIA
• Found as surface patches in tropical and humid biomes -- thin coverage but broad extent.
• Breaks into distinctive pea-sized pellets, visually unlike any other ore you'll encounter at surface.

ore_limestone.summary: Steelmaking flux; cement and concrete backbone; soil pH neutralizer; lime source.
ore_limestone.loreShort: Sedimentary carbonate rock from marine shelves and reefs; often fossil-bearing; can form karst caves and host replacement ores.
ore_limestone.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Gray to cream massive rock, often with fossils visible on weathered surfaces.
• Fresh break looks sugary-crystalline; reacts with acid if present.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Formation-scale layers spanning many kilometers; cliffs and plateaus; enormous quarries.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Karst terrain: sinkholes, caves, and springs. Sheer light-colored cliffs; thin soils on outcrop.

IN CONDUVIA
• Large continuous light-gray layers forming cliffs and cave systems.
• Fossil imprints on exposed faces are a clear identifier -- look for them on freshly broken surfaces.

ore_silica.summary: Glass and foundry sand; ceramics; silicon pipeline starter; quartzite as a high-grade silica source.
ore_silica.loreShort: Quartz-rich sands from beaches and dunes and clean sandstones; quartzite is metamorphosed sandstone; quartz veins also common.
ore_silica.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Sand: pale cream or golden gritty grains; often carries a glassy sparkle.
• Quartzite: hard, sugary crystalline rock; quartz crystals: clear or milky hexagonal prisms.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Dune fields can be vast; sandstones hundreds of meters thick; quartzite ridges run for kilometers.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Light-colored beaches and river sands; quartzite stands as resistant ridges; quartz veins cut across rock as white ribbons.

IN CONDUVIA
• Appears in two main forms: loose sand patches along riverbeds and desert flats, and quartz crystal veins cutting through rock.
• River-gathered sand is easier to access; vein quartz yields higher purity material.

ore_flint.summary: Stone Age toolmaking material; fire-starting sparks; sharp edges from conchoidal fracture.
ore_flint.loreShort: Microcrystalline quartz in chalk and limestone nodules and chert beds; often of biological silica origin in marine sediments.
ore_flint.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Dark gray, brown, or black waxy luster; conchoidal fracture produces razor-sharp edges.
• Often has a white chalky outer rind (cortex) if derived from nodules.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Nodules: golf ball to football size; bedded chert forms thin but laterally persistent layers.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Black nodules in white chalk; sharp flakes scattered on ground near chalk and gravel exposures.
• Resistant chert ribs outcrop where surrounding limestone has eroded away.

IN CONDUVIA
• Small black and brown nodules embedded in limestone and chalk layers.
• Striking flint against another hard stone produces sharp shards -- useful for early tools and firestarting.

ore_obsidian.summary: Ultra-sharp blade material; volcanic glass formed by rapid cooling of silica-rich lava.
ore_obsidian.loreShort: Forms at rhyolite domes and flow margins where lava cools too fast to crystallize; often near pumice and light rhyolite.
ore_obsidian.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Jet black glassy rock; conchoidal fracture; extremely sharp edges; brittle.
• Can be banded or bubble-rich; reflective in direct light.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Flows and domes can be hill-sized; outcrops meters to hundreds of meters thick and kilometers long.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Black glossy blocky flow surfaces; minimal vegetation on fresh flows; abundant sharp shards on the ground.

IN CONDUVIA
• Found as jet-black glassy boulders and flow surfaces in volcanic terrain.
• The edges of freshly broken obsidian are exceptionally sharp -- a prized early cutting material.
• Rare but unmistakable once you've seen it.

ore_native_copper.summary: The earliest copper route -- cold-workable metal nuggets and masses; later smelted.
ore_native_copper.loreShort: Occurs as native metal in basalt flows and sediment-hosted beds, and in oxidized zones of copper systems; often coated with green and blue copper minerals.
ore_native_copper.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Reddish metallic masses or wire forms; tarnish to green or black with age; malachite and azurite coatings common.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Nuggets from gram weight to fist size in gravels; rare giant masses have been found historically in certain districts.
• Can also occur as disseminations or plates within host rock.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Green malachite staining with a metallic red glint; heavy and malleable if hammered.

IN CONDUVIA
• Found near basalt cliffs or in river gravels as reddish metallic masses.
• Green malachite and blue azurite staining marks copper-bearing ground -- learn to read these weathering colors.

ore_bog_iron.summary: Early iron source from wetlands; bloomery-friendly; renewable on long timescales.
ore_bog_iron.loreShort: Forms by precipitation of iron oxyhydroxides in bogs where iron-rich groundwater meets oxygenated, acidic surface conditions.
ore_bog_iron.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Rust-brown earthy nodules and crusts; often porous and mixed with organic material.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Surficial deposit in peat and sand near the water table; nodules range from pea to fist size.
• Deposit regenerates over decades -- a slow renewable resource.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Orange sediment visible in bog water; rusty soils; an oily-looking surface film from iron bacteria is a reliable tell.

IN CONDUVIA
• Gathered from bogs and wetland areas -- not mined from seams but scooped from iron-rich ground.
• A reliable early iron source before deeper deposits become accessible.

tasks.desc: Obtain 12 raw cassiterite ore

ore_cassiterite.summary: The primary tin ore; essential for bronze alloy. Found as heavy dark pebbles in stream gravels.
ore_cassiterite.loreShort: Cassiterite (SnO₂) forms in high-temperature granite-related veins and pegmatites, then concentrates in placer gravels downstream -- the reason Bronze Age traders followed rivers to find it.
ore_cassiterite.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Heavy, dark brown to black; sub-metallic lustre; short stubby prisms or rounded stream pebbles.
• Very dense -- sinks fast and settles ahead of lighter material in moving water; often associated with quartz and tourmaline.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Hard-rock lodes (veins, pegmatites): narrow high-grade shoots.
• Alluvial placers: thin gravel lenses in stream beds and terrace gravels.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Dark heavy pebbles in clear-water stream gravels; black sand association with ilmenite and magnetite.
• Upstream hard-rock source: quartz-tourmaline veins in granite terrain.

IN CONDUVIA
• Shows up first as heavy dark pebbles in stream gravels -- the easier early find.
• Hard-rock vein deposits go deeper underground and offer a more consistent long-term source.

ore_spodumene.summary: Lithium for advanced batteries; also used in ceramics and glass inputs.
ore_spodumene.loreShort: Forms in granitic pegmatites (very coarse-grained dikes) with quartz, feldspar, and mica; crystals can be enormous.
ore_spodumene.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Large prismatic crystals; pale green, pink, or white; distinctive blade-like cleavage.
• Pegmatite host is visibly coarse: oversized feldspar and mica.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Pegmatite dikes 1–20 m thick, hundreds of meters long; ore shoots tens of meters.
• Crystal size can be extreme in rare cases -- meter-scale specimens exist.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Coarse "chunky granite" outcrops; occasional greenish blades visible on exposed surfaces.

IN CONDUVIA
• Found in mountain pegmatite veins, identified by unusually coarse, oversized crystal structure.
• If the granite-like rock around you has abnormally large mineral grains, you may be standing on a pegmatite.

ore_ree.summary: Rare earth elements for magnets, lasers, and electronics; a critical late-tech supply chain.
ore_ree.loreShort: Commonly from carbonatite intrusions and some hydrothermal systems; also from ionic adsorption clays and heavy mineral concentrates.
ore_ree.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Often subtle: brown or tan host rock with disseminated minerals; heavy mineral sands can include brownish grains.
• Not reliably distinctive-looking -- identification often requires instruments.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Carbonatite pits can be large; clay deposits blanket hillsides (meters thick over kilometers).

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Carbonate igneous rock context; barite and fluorite associations; slight radioactivity in some deposits.

IN CONDUVIA
• Rare earth ore won't announce itself visually -- this is anomaly-hunting territory.
• Use your scanner. The host rock and subtle mineral associations are the tell, not the ore itself.

ore_uraninite.summary: Uranium ore for nuclear fuel and late-game power chains; requires careful handling.
ore_uraninite.loreShort: Occurs in hydrothermal veins, sandstone roll-front deposits, and unconformity settings; commonly associated with alteration, sulfides, and redox boundaries.
ore_uraninite.notes:
VISUAL APPEARANCE
• Black pitchy massive material; can appear as a black smear or coating on surrounding rock.
• Bright secondary minerals often appear nearby: yellow-green crusts and stains.

TYPICAL SIZE / SCALE
• Veins: cm to tens of cm thick; roll-front bodies can be broad and at depth; unconformity pods can be small but very high grade.

LANDMARK / RECOGNITION
• Yellow staining on sandstone fractures.
• Often not visually obvious until you know what to look for -- instruments are the reliable method.

IN CONDUVIA
• Instruments are your primary tool here -- visual cues alone are unreliable.
• Yellow-green crust clusters around a dark core are what you're looking for on exposed faces.
