nodes.title: Automate a Line with a PLC

solid_state_chapter_intro.summary: Introduces Tier 5 as the moment your heavy-electric plant learns solid-state electronics, programmable controllers, CNC and the first industrial robots.
solid_state_chapter_intro.loreShort: From the late 1940s onward, transistors, integrated circuits, PLCs, CNC and industrial robots shifted factories from pure power problems to information and control problems.
solid_state_chapter_intro.notes: Gate this chapter on the AC and automation checkpoint token; auto-complete when the player opens it after finishing Tier 4.

solid_state_discrete_semiconductors.summary: Set up a small electronics bench to make basic silicon diodes, rectifier bridges and low-power transistors for switching and amplification.
solid_state_discrete_semiconductors.loreShort: The first working transistor was demonstrated at Bell Labs in 1947, replacing bulky, fragile vacuum tubes with a compact solid-state switch that could amplify and toggle signals for radios and early computers.
solid_state_discrete_semiconductors.notes: Treat this as the unlock for solid-state parts: after this node, power rectification, sensor interfaces and small-signal amplification can all use diodes and transistors instead of only mechanical or tube devices.

tasks.desc: Have or place a glass electronics bench.
Process raw silicon wafers on the bench.
Produce diodes, rectifier bridges and transistors.

solid_state_logic_modules.summary: Assemble small logic modules that bundle transistors and diodes into reusable AND, OR, NOT and flip-flop building blocks for control circuits.
solid_state_logic_modules.loreShort: Once discrete transistors existed, engineers wired them into logic gates and flip-flops, creating the basis for digital electronics, counters and early computers.
solid_state_logic_modules.notes: Use this quest to introduce boolean logic explicitly; players craft physical gate modules that later PLCs, counters or safety interlocks can be built from or conceptually reference.

tasks.desc: Craft AND/OR logic modules from transistors and diodes.
Craft NOT gates and flip-flop modules.
Have electromagnetic relays for hybrid control circuits.

solid_state_plc_rack.summary: Build a basic programmable logic controller rack with a CPU and several I/O modules that can scan inputs and drive outputs in a fixed cycle.
solid_state_plc_rack.loreShort: The first commercial PLCs, such as the Modicon 084 in the late 1960s, replaced huge banks of hardwired relays in auto plants with a rugged programmable controller.
solid_state_plc_rack.notes: Represent PLC behaviour as a simple scan loop that reads inputs, executes a ladder-style program and updates outputs, so players feel the jump from rewiring relays to editing logic.

tasks.desc: Have or place a PLC rack with CPU and I/O modules.
Install a switchboard panel with metering instruments.

solid_state_plc_line_automation.summary: Use your PLC to fully automate at least one production line, handling start, stop, interlocks and alarms without manual switch juggling.
solid_state_plc_line_automation.loreShort: In real factories, PLCs took over from relay logic to sequence conveyors, valves, presses and safety interlocks, especially in automotive and process plants from the 1970s onward.
solid_state_plc_line_automation.notes: Tie this node to a real in-game line, such as ore crushing or rolling; the reward token marks that at least one full loop now runs under programmable digital control instead of hand-operated switches.

tasks.desc: Write and load program cartridges for the PLC.
Install relays and alarm bells for interlock circuits.
Place level and speed sensors on the automated line.

solid_state_cnc_machine_shop.summary: Upgrade your machine shop with a CNC mill and CNC lathe that follow stored programs to cut parts to precise dimensions. This only works once you have stable power, precision measurement, and standardized fasteners.
solid_state_cnc_machine_shop.loreShort: Numerical control appeared in the 1940s and 1950s and evolved into full computer numerical control (CNC) by the 1970s, radically improving precision and repeatability in machining.
solid_state_cnc_machine_shop.notes: Model CNC as machines that consume program tickets or job recipes to output higher-tolerance parts for later high-tech tiers. Make sure players feel the dependency chain: standard gauges, quality tooling, and repeatable power before CNC reliability is believable.

tasks.desc: Have or place a CNC mill and CNC lathe.
Have an electric machine shop and industrial computer online.

solid_state_robot_cell.summary: Install a simple programmable robot arm in a fenced cell to load and unload hot or repetitive work from a machine, such as a press or CNC station.
solid_state_robot_cell.loreShort: The first successful industrial robot, Unimate, went into service at a General Motors plant in 1961, unloading hot die-cast parts and later handling welding and other hazardous, repetitive jobs.
solid_state_robot_cell.notes: Make this cell visibly safer and more repeatable than manual handling. Gate the robot behind CNC/precision machining, standardized fasteners, and a stable plant power system so automation feels earned rather than magical.

tasks.desc: Have or place a robot arm with a safety cage.
Install a powered conveyor and position sensors for the cell.

solid_state_scada_panel.summary: Add a digital overview panel and simple HMI screens that collect status from PLCs and sensors, so you can watch lines and acknowledge alarms from a central point.
solid_state_scada_panel.loreShort: From the late 20th century onward, SCADA and HMI systems let operators supervise entire plants from control rooms, with trends, alarm lists and status screens instead of just hardwired lamps and gauges.
solid_state_scada_panel.notes: Hook this into at least one existing production line and one power feeder so the player experiences the shift from walking the floor to supervising via screens and mimic panels.

tasks.desc: Have or place HMI panels and a rugged industrial computer.
Install the AC grid mimic panel with metering instruments.

solid_state_checkpoint.summary: Confirm that discrete semiconductors, logic modules, PLC racks, at least one PLC-run line, CNC machines, robot cells and digital plant monitoring are all running together.
solid_state_checkpoint.loreShort: By the late 20th century, factories combined solid-state electronics, PLCs, CNC tools, industrial robots and SCADA systems into tightly integrated automated plants, with information flow as important as steam or steel.
solid_state_checkpoint.notes: Use this checkpoint as the hard gate into Tier 6, where your now-digital factory extends into space, high-energy systems and offworld logistics built on top of everything learned here.

solid_state_silicon_upgrade.summary: Upgrade from raw silicon wafers to device-grade monocrystalline wafers by building the full trichlorosilane / Siemens CVD / Czochralski pull sequence: MG-Si + HCl → SiHCl3 → (CVD) → 9N poly → (CZ pull) → ingot → (wire saw + CMP) → polished wafer.
solid_state_silicon_upgrade.loreShort: The Siemens trichlorosilane process (commercialised 1954) and Czochralski crystal pulling (Teal & Little, 1950) made megawatt-scale silicon electronics possible by achieving six more orders of magnitude of purity over metallurgical grade.
solid_state_silicon_upgrade.notes: Gate: requires silicon_mg_grade and gas_hcl (both from Electric chemistry gateway). Outputs polished_wafer used by logic_chip and cell_photovoltaic.

tasks.desc: React MG-Si with HCl gas to produce trichlorosilane (SiHCl3).
Run the Siemens CVD bell-jar reactor to deposit 9N polysilicon on hot rods.
Pull a Czochralski monocrystalline ingot and saw it into polished device-grade wafers.

solid_state_logic_chips.summary: Fabricate integrated circuit logic chips from polished Czochralski wafers: define active regions by ion implantation, grow gate oxide, pattern and etch multiple metal interconnect layers by photolithography, then dice into individual chips.
solid_state_logic_chips.loreShort: The first commercial silicon ICs appeared in the early 1960s (Fairchild, TI). By 1971, the Intel 4004 placed a complete CPU on one chip, beginning digital control of everything from calculators to rocket guidance computers.
solid_state_logic_chips.notes: Logic chips feed avionics_flight_computer (Space tier) and all high-end digital control systems. Recipe: silicon_wafer_polished + copper_wire_drawn @ machine_shop_electric.

tasks.desc: Fab logic chips from polished wafers and copper interconnects.

solid_state_carbon_fiber.summary: Produce carbon fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP) structural panels: coal tar → acrylonitrile (Sohio process) → polyacrylonitrile (PAN) fiber → stabilize → carbonize at 1200°C → impregnate with epoxy → autoclave cure at 130-180°C.
solid_state_carbon_fiber.loreShort: Aerospace-grade PAN-based carbon fiber (Toray T300, 1971) transformed aircraft and rocket structures: 5x stronger per kilogram than steel with zero corrosion. CFRP composite tanks and fairings are structural requirements for orbital launch vehicles.
solid_state_carbon_fiber.notes: Inputs: coal_tar, gas_ammonia, sodium_hydroxide (all from earlier tiers). Output cfrp_panel is a required structural material for the Space-tier rocket airframe.

tasks.desc: Produce acrylonitrile via the Sohio process, then polymerize and spin PAN fiber.
Stabilize PAN fiber and carbonize at 1200°C in nitrogen to form carbon fiber tow.
Produce epoxy resin and autoclave-cure CFRP structural panels.

solidstate_token_convert.title: Trade In: Tech V → VI
solidstate_token_convert.summary: Exchange Tier V tech tokens for Tier VI. Repeatable.

nodes.title: Robotic Lander and Lunar ISRU Demo
Mass Driver for Bulk Lunar Exports
Tier 6 -- Space Age and High-Energy Online

space_chapter_intro.summary:
### Tier 6 is where the factory looks outward

You have an automated, solid-state industrial base.
It produces electricity, refined materials, and precision components without you touching most of it.

Tier 6 uses that base to do something no earlier tier could: leave.

The arc of this chapter:

- **Propellants and test stands** -- liquid-fuel rocketry. Kerosene and LOX. Instrumented engine runs before anything leaves the ground.
- **First orbital launch** -- a three-stage rocket placing hardware in orbit. Not a suborbital hop. Actual orbit.
- **Satellite network** -- communications and sensing infrastructure. Your world gains persistent orbital coverage.
- **Nuclear base-load power** -- the grid problem at this scale is real. Nuclear solves it without burning anything.
- **Orbital station** -- orbit becomes a place where work happens, not just a trajectory.
- **Lunar ISRU demo** -- a robotic lander extracts resources in-situ. The Moon starts feeding the supply chain.
- **Mass driver** -- bulk lunar material launched without rockets. Energy cost drops by an order of magnitude.
- **Orbital solar power** -- the grid reaches into space. Base-load from orbit, beamed down.

This is only possible because T3 through T5 automated the supply chain that feeds it.
Without the blast furnace chain, the ore processing line, the solid-state fab -- none of this lifts off.
space_chapter_intro.loreShort: From the mid-20th century onward, rocketry, nuclear power and the Space Age turned industrial engineering outward into orbit, the Moon and beyond.
space_chapter_intro.notes: Gate this chapter on the solid-state checkpoint token; it auto-completes once the player opens the chapter after finishing Tier 5.

space_propellants_test_stand.summary: Refine kerosene and liquid oxygen, build a vertical engine test stand and fire modern liquid-fuel rocket engines under instrumentation.
space_propellants_test_stand.loreShort: Orbital-class launchers routinely burn liquid oxygen with refined kerosene (RP-1) or similar fuels, and their engines are qualified on static test stands before flight.
space_propellants_test_stand.notes: Make this node require at least one full-duration static test with sensors and a visible plume so the player feels how much setup a single engine needs.

tasks.desc: Have or place a vertical engine test stand.
Produce and load propellants for test firing.
Fire kerolox test engines with high-pressure instruments.

space_orbital_launch.summary: Construct a launch pad and three-stage rocket capable of placing a small uncrewed satellite into low orbit, then prove you can reach space reliably.
space_orbital_launch.loreShort: The Space Age opened with multi-stage rockets like Sputnik 1's launcher in 1957, showing that stacked liquid-fuel stages were the way to reach orbit.
space_orbital_launch.notes: Require at least one successful orbital insertion and tracking pass for the test satellite so the player learns staging, guidance and ground support all matter.

tasks.desc: Have or place a launch pad complex and tracking radar.
Assemble a three-stage orbital rocket with a tech demo satellite.

space_satellite_network.summary: Deploy a small constellation of communication or relay satellites and tie them into ground stations so your world has continuous coverage over key regions.
space_satellite_network.loreShort: Since the 1960s, communication and weather satellites have transformed navigation, broadcasting and forecasting by staying in orbit and linking distant ground points.
space_satellite_network.notes: Hook this into in-game networks so players see real benefits: remote outposts can receive control signals or telemetry once the satcom network token is online.

tasks.desc: Launch three communication satellites into orbit.
Have or place ground stations, tracking radar and a relay station.

space_nuclear_grid.summary: Build a grid-connected nuclear power station that can provide steady base-load electricity for launches and heavy industry without relying on fossil fuels.
space_nuclear_grid.loreShort: Civil nuclear power plants, first feeding grids in the 1950s, became major base-load sources that support industry with long-running, high-output reactors.
space_nuclear_grid.notes: Model realistic constraints: long start-up, high output, careful cooling and safety interlocks, so nuclear feels powerful but not trivial.

tasks.desc: Have or place a light water reactor and cooling towers.
Load enriched uranium fuel rods into the reactor.
Connect the reactor output to a high-voltage switchyard.

space_orbital_station.summary: Assemble a modular orbital station with power, life support and docking, turning orbit from a fly-through into a place where work can happen continuously.
space_orbital_station.loreShort: Space stations from Salyut and Skylab through Mir and the ISS enabled long-duration human presence in orbit and microgravity research unavailable on the ground.
space_orbital_station.notes: Have this node unlock an orbital map layer where the player can place microgravity labs and storage, supplied by launch vehicles from the surface.

tasks.desc: Launch and assemble the core station module with solar trusses.
Install docking ports and a life support loop.

space_lunar_isru_probe.summary: Send an uncrewed lander to a lunar site, drill regolith or ice, run a small in-situ resource utilisation demo and return data or samples to orbit or ground.
space_lunar_isru_probe.loreShort: Modern Moon and Mars mission plans emphasise robotic prospecting and ISRU, using local ice and regolith to support bases instead of lifting everything from Earth.
space_lunar_isru_probe.notes: Make this play like a remote mission: the player configures the lander and drill chain on the ground, then watches a mission sequence and receives resources or proof tokens back later.

tasks.desc: Build a robotic lunar lander with a regolith drill.
Equip the lander with an ISRU demo unit and sample return capsule.

space_mass_driver_lunar.summary: Construct a prototype electromagnetic mass driver on the Moon to fling processed regolith or ore pods into lunar orbit instead of launching them by rocket.
space_mass_driver_lunar.loreShort: Since the 1970s, space-settlement studies have proposed lunar or asteroid mass drivers -- essentially electric railguns -- as ways to lift bulk material into space using local power.
space_mass_driver_lunar.notes: Tie this into your logistics: once the driver is online, bulk lunar materials can arrive in orbit as scheduled payload packets rather than per-rocket launches.

tasks.desc: Build the mass driver track with guidance coil arrays.
Install a lunar solar power plant and bulk regolith hopper.

space_orbital_solar_power.summary: Build a solar power satellite with large arrays in orbit that beams energy down to a rectenna field, supplementing your ground grid with space-based power.
space_orbital_solar_power.loreShort: Space-based solar power concepts since the late 1960s have proposed collecting sunlight in orbit and transmitting it to Earth via microwaves or lasers for near-continuous supply.
space_orbital_solar_power.notes: Balance this against nuclear: orbital power offers clean but launch-mass-hungry infrastructure, while reactors give dense on-planet output; both feed the same industrial grid.

tasks.desc: Build and launch a solar power satellite with attitude control.
Install the microwave beamer and ground rectenna field.

space_checkpoint.summary: Confirm that liquid-fuel rocketry, orbital launch, satellite networks, nuclear base-load power, an orbital station, lunar ISRU, a mass driver and space-based solar power are all operating together in your ecosystem.
space_checkpoint.loreShort: By the early 21st century, humanity combined mature rocketry, satellites, nuclear grids and serious studies of lunar resources, mass drivers and orbital solar power into a high-energy, space-facing industrial system.
space_checkpoint.notes: Use this checkpoint as the hard gate into the next tier of exotic materials and fusion-heavy space industry.

space_titanium_chain.summary: Process ilmenite ore through the full titanium chain: fluidized-bed chlorination (TiO2 + 2Cl2 + 2C → TiCl4 + 2CO) → fractional distillation → Hunter reduction (TiCl4 + 4Na → Ti + 4NaCl) → vacuum arc remelting (VAR, triple-melt for rotating parts) → Ti-6Al-4V alloying → plate forging and rolling.
space_titanium_chain.loreShort: Ti-6Al-4V combines ~1100 MPa tensile strength, low density, and full corrosion resistance against RP-1 and LOX -- the standard material for turbopump housings, pressure vessel domes, structural bulkheads, and heat shields in launch vehicles.
space_titanium_chain.notes: Requires: raw_ore_ilmenite (ore node Step 0), gas_chlorine (Electric chapter), coke_fuel (Steel tier), sodium_metal (Downs cell from Electric chapter).

tasks.desc: Chlorinate ilmenite ore in a fluidized bed to produce TiCl4 ('tickle').
Run Hunter reduction (TiCl4 + 4Na) and vacuum arc remelt the sponge.
VAR-alloy Ti-6Al-4V and hot-roll titanium plate for structural use.

space_refractory_metals.summary: Produce refractory and strategic metals: wolframite → NaOH pressure digest → solvent extraction → APT precipitation → calcine WO3 → H2 reduction → W powder → press/sinter/swage W rod; and columbite → HF digest → MIBK solvent extraction → Nb2O5 calcine → aluminothermic reduction → ferroniobium.
space_refractory_metals.loreShort: Tungsten (3422u00b0C melting point, highest of any element) lines rocket nozzle throats where nothing else survives combined temperature, pressure, and abrasive exhaust. Ferroniobium (65% Nb) grain-refines stainless steel and provides 5% of Inconel 718, the standard turbopump disc and blade alloy.
space_refractory_metals.notes: Requires: raw_ore_wolframite, raw_ore_columbite (ore Step 0), sodium_hydroxide, acid_hydrofluoric, gas_hydrogen, metal_aluminum_ingot.

tasks.desc: Digest wolframite in NaOH, solvent-extract, and calcine APT to produce WO3.
H2-reduce WO3 to W powder, cold-press, sinter at 2300u00b0C, and swage into dense W rods.
HF-digest columbite, MIBK-extract Nb, calcine to Nb2O5, and aluminothermically reduce to ferroniobium.

space_zircaloy_inconel.summary: Complete the high-performance alloy matrix. Zirconium chain: zircon → carbo-chlorination → ZrCl4 → Hf separation (< 100 ppm Hf for nuclear grade) → Kroll reduction → vacuum-induction melt → zircaloy-2 cladding alloy (Zr + 1.3% Sn + 0.1% Fe + 0.1% Cr). Superalloy chain: nickel cathode + ferrochrome + steel_ingot + ferroniobium → VIM then VAR → Inconel 718-like ingot.
space_zircaloy_inconel.loreShort: Zircaloy is transparent to thermal neutrons, strong at 350u00b0C in reactor water, and corrosion-proof -- the only viable nuclear fuel rod cladding material. Inconel 718 retains full strength at 700u00b0C under a cryogenic propellant differential, making it the standard for turbopump rotating components, combustion chamber liners, and exhaust manifolds in liquid-fuel engines.
space_zircaloy_inconel.notes: Requires: raw_ore_zircon (ore Step 0), sodium_metal (Downs cell), nickel_cathode (Electric tier), ferrochrome, steel_ingot, ferroniobium (this chapter).

tasks.desc: Carbo-chlorinate zircon to ZrCl4 and Kroll-reduce to hafnium-free sponge.
Vacuum-induction melt zircaloy-2 from Hf-free sponge (nuclear-grade fuel rod cladding).
VIM + VAR melt Inconel 718-like superalloy from nickel cathode + ferrochrome + ferroniobium.

space_token_convert.title: Trade In: Tech VI → VII
space_token_convert.summary: Exchange Tier VI tech tokens for Tier VII. Repeatable.

steam.title: Tier 2 -- Coal, Waterpower, and Early Steam

nodes.title: Atmospheric Pump for a Wet Shaft

steam_chapter_intro.summary:
### Tier 2 is where "site" starts to matter

You are no longer just a camp with better tools.
You are a **works**:

- waterpower with line-shaft distribution
- coal access (the fuel that breaks woodland limits)
- coke production for ironworks-grade heat
- a boiler house with a real steam loop
- your first serious steam use is mine drainage (pumping), then expanded into rotary shop drive

[hint]You need a **bronze pickaxe or iron hammer** (from the Metal Ages chapter) to mine coal and hematite.  Make sure you have one before tackling coal access.[/hint]
steam_chapter_intro.loreShort: Early industrial sites mixed older waterpower with coal-fired steam plants, scaling work beyond muscle and charcoal limits.
steam_chapter_intro.lore:
### Keep this tier honest

Steam starts as **stationary power**.
Not trains. Not magic torque.
Pumps first. Rotation second.
steam_chapter_intro.notes: Gated by metal_ages_III_complete; auto-completes on open.

steam_waterwheel_first.summary:
### Put a stream to work

Build an overshot wheel where you have **usable head** and couple it to a short shaft.

Waterpower is old -- and that's the point:
it's reliable when the site supports it.
steam_waterwheel_first.loreShort: Overshot wheels are chosen when you can drop water onto the wheel and convert head into torque efficiently.
steam_waterwheel_first.lore:
### What "good placement" means

- you need fall (head), not just flow
- you need a stable channel intake
- you need a safe spot for the rotating assembly

You could have built a wooden wheel centuries ago. You couldn't have built the iron shaft, stone millrace, and timber gearing that make it actually useful. Now you can.

tasks.desc: Have or place an overshot waterwheel.
Have a first short line shaft coupled to the wheel (basic mechanical output).

steam_water_workshop.summary:
### Line-shaft workshop bay

Extend shafting and use belts + sheaves to distribute power to tools.

This is the "factory floor" pattern:
one prime mover → many driven stations.
steam_water_workshop.loreShort: Line shafts and belt drives distribute power across a workshop long before electric motors.
steam_water_workshop.lore:
### What this unlocks

You stop asking:
"can I power this tool?"

You start asking:
"how many tools can this shaft feed?"

tasks.desc: Have additional shafting and belt drive parts ready.
Bring at least one driven tool online (air for fire or force for forming).

steam_coal_access.summary:
### Open the coal measures

Find and work a coal seam until you have a steady supply flowing to your works.

Coal changes scale:
it's why steam becomes practical on sites that outgrow woodland fuel.
steam_coal_access.loreShort: Coal access is the enabling resource behind stationary steam plants and large heat work.
steam_coal_access.lore:
### Reality check

Mines flood.
That problem is why early steam engines get built at all.

tasks.desc: Mine bituminous coal from a seam and deliver it to your works.

steam_coke_ovens.summary:
### Make coke for industrial heat

Convert coal into coke: hotter, cleaner-burning, and suited for ironworks-scale firing.

This is the fuel bridge between "some coal" and "serious heat on demand".

[hint]**Don't skip the coal buffer.**  Producing 40 coke consumes roughly 80 coal, and your blast furnace (built in the Steel chapter, but usable now) will need even more.  The 80-coal stock task below is not optional -- treat it as the minimum working supply.[/hint]
steam_coke_ovens.loreShort: Coke supports higher, steadier heat for heavy firing and large ironworking compared to raw coal's smoke/impurities in many uses.
steam_coke_ovens.lore:
### About the oven form

Early coke can be made in clamps/heaps; beehive ovens are a later high-throughput form.
Functionally, the point is the same: controlled off-gassing to turn coal into coke.

### Coal ratio reality

The coal→coke conversion is roughly 2:1 by mass in a beehive oven.
40 coke costs you ~80 coal.  When the blast furnace is running it will consume coke
continuously -- keep your coal supply well ahead of your coke demand.
steam_coke_ovens.notes: If you want stricter chronology, treat the beehive oven as a stand-in for early clamp/heap coke burning.

tasks.desc: Have or place a coke oven.
Run a first coke burn and produce a starter batch.
Keep 80 coal on hand -- this supply will be consumed making the coke you already burned plus the next batch.

steam_boiler_and_pipes.summary:
### Low-pressure boiler house

Assemble a conservative stationary boiler plant:
shell, firebox, pipes, feedwater loop, condensation/cooling hardware, and basic safety.

This is **low-pressure practice**:
enough to run early engines, not a racing locomotive.

[hint]The cast-iron firebox and cylinder both require **pig iron**.  Pig iron needs a small blast furnace (refractory bricks + coke + limestone → blast_furnace_small).  Build that first -- hover `blast_furnace_small` and press **R** for the recipe.[/hint]

[hint]**Limestone gotcha:** mining limestone nodes yields `raw_ore_limestone` (the geologic ore form). The blast furnace recipe expects `limestone_rock` (discrete nodules). Convert at hand with `raw_ore_limestone -> limestone_rock` (1:1, free). Same trick applies to most "raw_ore_X" + "X_rock" pairs.[/hint]
steam_boiler_and_pipes.loreShort: Stationary steam plants stand or fall on water management and safety discipline: feedwater, pressure relief, and level awareness.
steam_boiler_and_pipes.lore:
### Non-negotiables

- you must control pressure (relief)
- you must control water level (don't expose iron to direct fire dry)
- you must move water back in (feedwater loop)

### Pig iron in the Steam era

Early boiler makers used small cupola-style furnaces long before the large Bessemer converters
of the Steel age.  Your `blast_furnace_small` recipe reflects this -- it is buildable now, not
gated behind the Steel chapter.  Build it, charge it with coke + hematite + limestone, and tap
enough pig iron to cast the firebox and cylinder.

### Why this gate exists

Safe pressure vessels require **repeatable metallurgy, tight machining tolerances, and standardized fasteners**.
If you can't make consistent plates, rivets, and seals, you don't get steam.

tasks.desc: Have the boiler shell + cast-iron firebox ready. (Firebox needs pig iron -- build a blast_furnace_small first if you haven't.)
Have piping + feedwater hardware ready for a complete loop.
Have basic safety + level indication online.

steam_mine_pump.summary:
### Atmospheric pump for a wet shaft

Put steam to work where it historically earns its keep first:
**mine drainage**.

Build a simple pumping engine set (atmospheric-style behavior, condensation-driven cycle)
and show it can keep a wet shaft workable.
steam_mine_pump.loreShort: The first widely successful steam engines were built to pump water out of mines so deeper seams could be worked.
steam_mine_pump.lore:
### What makes it "early steam"

It's not about high pressure.
It's about cycling steam and condensation to drive a piston and pump rod reliably.

tasks.desc: Have the basic engine + cylinder assembly ready. (Cast-iron cylinder needs pig iron from your blast furnace.)
Have condensation + sealing materials ready (cycle reliability).

steam_rotative_engine.summary:
### Turn steam into rotation

Upgrade from "pumps only" to **general drive**:
add flywheel, rotary conversion, speed regulation, and belt/shaft outputs.

Now the steam plant can drive the same shop logic as waterpower:
shafts, belts, and stations.
steam_rotative_engine.loreShort: Industrial steam becomes transformative when it can deliver smooth rotary motion and regulate speed under changing load.
steam_rotative_engine.lore:
### What changes

- flywheel smooths cyclic power
- rotary conversion enables continuous drives
- governor prevents runaway and stabilizes machine work

tasks.desc: Have the rotary power train parts ready.
Have belts/pulleys/shafting ready to distribute steam power into a workshop.

steam_stamp_mill.summary:
### Mechanised stamp milling

Connect your powertrain to an ore crushing station.
Cams lift and drop stamps to break raw ore into fine feed for furnaces.

Route hematite from your ore supply into the mill input.
Crushed ore flows out the other side.

This is throughput -- not glamour.
steam_stamp_mill.loreShort: Stamping is a long-used mechanical crushing method; power makes it scalable and consistent.
steam_stamp_mill.lore:
### Why this sits here

Your ironwork stops being "small batch heroic"
and becomes "repeatable processing".

That is the real steam-era shift.

tasks.desc: Have or place a stamp mill assembly connected to your powertrain.
Feed raw hematite ore to the stamp mill input.
Produce crushed ore output from the stamp mill.

steam_checkpoint.summary:
### Tier 2 complete -- Steam Age online

You have a real early-industrial works package:

- waterwheel + line-shaft workshop distribution
- coal access as a sustained fuel supply
- coke production as an industrial heat chain
- a conservative boiler house + steam loop discipline
- steam justified first by mine drainage, then extended into rotary shop power
- mechanised ore crushing throughput (stamp milling)

Next: Steel takes this into **high steam** and **DC power**.
steam_checkpoint.loreShort: A site that can move water, fuel, heat, and rotary power reliably is ready for steel-era scale and electrification logic.
steam_checkpoint.lore:
### The line this draws

From here forward, the game can assume:
- prime movers exist (water + steam)
- fuel is industrial (coal + coke chain)
- power distribution exists (shafts/belts now; wires later)
- ore prep is mechanised

That's the on-ramp to high-pressure steam and electrical systems.

steam_token_convert.title: Trade In: Industry II → III
steam_token_convert.summary: Exchange Tier II industry tokens for Tier III. Repeatable.

nodes.title: From Steam Ironworks to Steel & Power

steel_chapter_intro.summary:
### From Steam Ironworks to Steel & Power

Your steam plant has proven the principle: controlled heat, moving pressure, turning shafts.

Tier 3 asks you to scale all of it.

- **Blast furnace** replaces your bloomery and wrought-iron forge with coke-fired pig iron production
- **Bessemer and open-hearth converters** turn pig iron into *steel* -- harder, tougher, weldable
- **Rolled products** become structural material for bridges, ships, and boiler shells
- **High-pressure boilers** push your engines far beyond Tier 2 limits
- **Compound engines** do more work with less coal
- **Dynamos** are steam turned to electricity
- **DC networks** wire your site for the first time
- **Motors** put power exactly where you need it, not just where a shaft happens to run

This is the tier where your works stop being a collection of machines and start being a site.
steel_chapter_intro.loreShort: Historically, coke-fuelled blast furnaces, new steelmaking routes and steam-driven dynamos created dense industrial districts of mills, rails and lit streets.
steel_chapter_intro.notes: Gate on steam_III_complete; auto-complete once the player opens the chapter.

steel_coke_blast_furnace.summary:
### Coke blast furnace

Your bloomery reduced iron without melting it. This is different.

A coke blast furnace runs hot enough to produce *liquid* pig iron --
iron with 3–4% dissolved carbon, poured from a tap hole at the base.
It's not wrought iron. It's brittle cast metal that feeds the converters.

The hot-blast stove preheats the incoming air, cutting coke consumption
by 30–40% compared to cold-blast operation. It's one of the most
important efficiency upgrades in 19th-century metallurgy.

Load ore, coke, and limestone flux in alternating layers from the top.
Tap pig iron from the bottom. The furnace runs continuously -- never cools.

[hint]**Plan ahead: this chapter needs ~200+ pig iron total.**
This quest proves the furnace works (40 pig iron).
The Bessemer quest that follows consumes 80, and the open-hearth quest consumes another 80.
Keep your blast furnace running between quests and build a stockpile before you start converting.[/hint]
steel_coke_blast_furnace.loreShort: Coke-fired blast furnaces made it possible to build taller stacks and produce pig iron at much larger scale than charcoal alone allowed.
steel_coke_blast_furnace.notes: The blast furnace is the first machine that produces pig iron -- the quest proves it works by requiring pig iron output, not pig iron input.

tasks.desc: Have or place the blast furnace and hot-blast stove.
Line the furnace with refractory bricks.
Feed crushed ore, coke and flux into the blast furnace.
Tap pig iron from the blast furnace to prove it works.

steel_bessemer_steel.summary:
### Bessemer converter: fast bulk steel

Pig iron is brittle because it contains too much carbon.
The Bessemer process fixes this in minutes, not hours.

Load the converter with molten pig iron.
Tilt it upright. Blast cold air through the tuyeres at the base.
The air burns out excess carbon -- the heat is generated by the carbon combustion,
not from external fuel. The heat is self-sustaining.

In 15–20 minutes: bulk steel.

Bessemer is fast and cheap. It's not precise -- composition control is poor.
Use it for rails and structural shapes where exact carbon content matters less.

[hint]**Pig iron demand for this chapter is high.** This quest alone consumes 80 pig iron, and the open-hearth furnace quest that follows consumes another 80.  Before you start, make sure your blast furnace can keep up -- tap and stockpile at least 200 pig iron before you begin converting.  Falling short mid-quest is frustrating.[/hint]
steel_bessemer_steel.loreShort: The Bessemer process blew air through molten pig iron to oxidise carbon and impurities, making steel much cheaper and faster to produce than before.
steel_bessemer_steel.notes: Model this as high-throughput but less flexible: great for bulk rail-grade steel when your blast furnace is running hard.

tasks.desc: Have or place a basic Bessemer converter.
Charge the converter with pig iron from your blast furnace.
Produce steel ingots from the Bessemer blow.

steel_open_hearth.summary:
### Siemens–Martin open-hearth furnace

Bessemer is fast. Open-hearth is careful.

The furnace uses *regenerators* -- alternating chambers of heated brickwork
that pre-heat combustion air by recycling exhaust heat. This lets the furnace
reach temperatures high enough to fully melt the charge rather than just blowing
air through it.

Full melting means you can add scrap, control the slag chemistry, and sample
the heat repeatedly before tapping. The result: better composition control
and higher-quality steel for pressure vessels and load-bearing structures.

Slower than Bessemer, but the furnace that built bridges, battleships, and boilers.
steel_open_hearth.loreShort: Siemens–Martin open-hearth furnaces reused exhaust heat to fully melt steel, refine big charges and recycle scrap with more precise composition control.
steel_open_hearth.notes: Position this as slower but tunable and scrap-friendly; Bessemer is your fast rail furnace, open-hearth your careful structural and plate shop.

tasks.desc: Have or place an open-hearth furnace.
Charge the open-hearth furnace with pig iron.
Produce refined steel ingots from the open-hearth melt.

steel_structural_products.summary:
### Rails, beams, and boiler plate

Steel is only useful when it can be shaped into precise structural forms.
A rolling mill squeezes hot steel between shaped rollers to produce uniform
cross-sections: rails, I-beams, angles, flat plate.

Rolling replaces forging for bulk production. The shapes are consistent.
The tolerances are tight enough to bolt together.

**What this unlocks:**
- Steel rails: your transport network scales with them
- Steel beams: buildings and bridges that don't rely on timber or cast iron
- Boiler plate: steel pressure vessels for the next stage of engine upgrades
- Gusset plates: structural joints that actually hold under dynamic load

The rolling mill is one of the loudest, most impressive machines in the works.
It should look like it.
steel_structural_products.loreShort: Cheap rolled steel rails, beams and plate underpinned railways, large bridges, ships and high-pressure boilers in late-19th-century industry.
steel_structural_products.notes: Have placement tasks reference at least one upgraded structure so players see the visual jump from timber-and-wrought to steel framing.

tasks.desc: Have or place a steam rolling mill.
Stock rolled products (rails, beams, plate, gussets).

steel_high_pressure_boilers.summary:
### Steel boilers: higher pressure

Your Tier 2 boilers ran on wrought iron plate at moderate pressure.
That's a hard ceiling -- wrought iron fatigues at high pressure cycles.

Steel plate is stronger, more consistent, and can be rolled thicker.
Riveted steel shells with proper stays can safely run at 2–3× the pressure
your old boilers could handle.

Higher pressure means the same mass of steam does more work per stroke.
Your engines are about to get significantly more powerful for the same fuel.

The weighted safety valve isn't cosmetic. Overpressure kills people and destroys sites.
steel_high_pressure_boilers.loreShort: Improved steel plate and design let boilers carry higher pressures, boosting engine power but demanding good safety valves and inspection routines.
steel_high_pressure_boilers.notes: Tie boiler rating and allowed engine load to this token so players feel why steel and maintenance matter for high-pressure steam.

tasks.desc: Have or place the riveted boiler shells.
Stock plate, lagging, pipe, and safety valves for the rebuild.

steel_compound_engines.summary:
### Compound engines: do more with the same steam

A simple engine uses steam once, then exhausts it at near-boiler pressure --
wasting most of its energy.

A compound engine uses steam in stages: a high-pressure cylinder takes the initial
expansion, then exhausts into a larger low-pressure cylinder for a second expansion.
Same boiler. Same coal. Significantly more shaft power.

The Corliss valve gear adds variable cut-off: steam admission stops partway
through the stroke, letting expansion do more work before exhaust.
This is the engine that made 19th-century mills competitive.

The flyball governor keeps shaft speed stable under varying load -- important
for workshops, rolling mills, and eventually dynamos.
steel_compound_engines.loreShort: Compound and Corliss-style mill engines expanded steam in stages and used better valve gear, cutting coal use per unit of shaft power.
steel_compound_engines.notes: Represent this as an efficiency buff: same shaft power for less fuel and water, plus a more stable speed for workshops and rolling mills.

tasks.desc: Have the required build items ready.

steel_dynamo_plant.summary:
### Steam-driven dynamo house

Your engines turn shafts. A dynamo turns a shaft into direct current.

The principle: coils of wire rotating in a magnetic field induce voltage.
Early dynamos use wrought iron cores for the field magnets
and laminated armature cores to reduce eddy current losses.

Belt-couple a reliable engine to two dynamos in a dedicated bay.
Wire the outputs to copper bus bars at the switchboard.
The machine shop now has electrical capability.

This is the moment your site produces electricity.
Everything that follows -- lights, motors, instruments -- is downstream of this.
steel_dynamo_plant.loreShort: Steam-driven dynamos in central stations supplied low-voltage DC for factories and street lighting before large AC grids existed.
steel_dynamo_plant.notes: Place the dynamo house near boilers and coal; emphasise shaft or belt coupling so the steam → motion → electricity chain is obvious.

tasks.desc: Have or place the dynamos and electric machine shop bay.
Stock magnet cores, armatures, and bus bars for the dynamo house.

steel_dc_network.summary:
### Low-voltage DC network and lights

Electricity is only useful when it goes somewhere.

Run insulated copper wire from your dynamo switchboard to lamp fixtures
across the works. The circuit is simple: dynamo → switchboard → fuse → switch → lamp.
The voltage drop over distance limits how far you can push a DC network --
this is not a long-distance transmission system, it's a local lighting and small-power grid.

The incandescent lamps use carbon filaments (1879-era technology).
They're fragile, short-lived, and dim by later standards.
But they are *electric light*. Your site can now operate after dark.

The wooden switchboard, knife switches, and rewireable fuses are early electrical
safety discipline. They are not decorative.
steel_dc_network.loreShort: Early DC systems used steam dynamos, wooden switchboards, knife switches and carbon-filament lamps to light compact districts at low voltage.
steel_dc_network.notes: Introduce basic electrical safety concepts: overcurrent protection, isolation switches and why voltage drop keeps early DC networks local.

tasks.desc: Have or place the switchboard panel and lamp fixtures.
Stock switches, fuses, insulators, wire, and lamps.

steel_dc_motors_workshop.summary:
### Motors on the shop floor

A DC motor is a dynamo run in reverse: send current through it and the shaft turns.

The first practical DC motors were installed in factories in the 1880s.
They did not immediately replace line shafting -- the transition was decades-long
and gradual. But they changed what was *possible*:

- A machine tool could now run independently of the main shaft
- A hoist could be placed exactly where lifting was needed
- A department could be stopped for maintenance without halting the whole plant

Install DC motors on at least four key tools or hoists.
Keep some shafting -- the transition is real, not instant.
The remaining shafting will disappear in Tier 4 and beyond.
steel_dc_motors_workshop.loreShort: DC motors derived from dynamo designs began to power machine tools and hoists, letting factories put power exactly where it was needed.
steel_dc_motors_workshop.notes: Treat motors as precise, per-machine power taps; some stations can now run when shafts are stopped or be isolated for maintenance more easily.

tasks.desc: Have the required build items ready.

steel_checkpoint.summary:
### Tier 3 online: steel and DC power

Your site is no longer a steam mill with iron parts.

It is a steel plant with electric light.

**Confirm what's running:**
- Coke blast furnace producing pig iron continuously
- Bessemer converter for bulk steel heats
- Open-hearth furnace for controlled, high-quality steel
- Rolling mill producing rails, beams, and boiler plate
- High-pressure riveted steel boilers
- Compound or mill engines with governors on the main shafts
- DC dynamo house with a working switchboard
- DC distribution network with lighting and fuses
- DC motors installed on key machines and hoists

This is the mature 1880s–1890s heavy industrial site.
Tier 4 adds AC, transformers, induction motors, vacuum tube electronics,
and the first centralised control systems.
steel_checkpoint.loreShort: This mix -- mass steel, powerful boilers and engines, plus DC power and motors -- matches late-19th-century heavy industry just before AC grids took over.
steel_checkpoint.notes: Hard gate into the Electric chapter; toast should recap that the site now runs a true steel-and-power complex.

steel_token_convert.title: Trade In: Industry III → IV
steel_token_convert.summary: Exchange Tier III industry tokens for Tier IV. Repeatable.

stone_chapter_intro.summary:
### Stone camp -- routine, buffers, and controlled heat

Still no metal. That's fine -- this tier is about turning your scrappy survival setup into something that actually runs:

- cordage + basic tools
- a **controlled hearth** with real fuel discipline
- charcoal for reliable high heat
- clay containers fired into proper ceramics
- dry storage and grain grinding
- food buffers + basic sanitation

[hint]If your camp runs on purpose, Tier 2 can start asking for real metallurgy.[/hint]
stone_chapter_intro.loreShort: Stone Age here means routines, buffers, and controlled fire -- not wandering around with a club.
stone_chapter_intro.lore:
### What changes between Tier 0 and Tier 1

Tier 0 proves you can survive a night.

Tier 1 proves you can **stay stable while doing actual work**:
- fewer emergency trips
- more stuff in reserve
- better heat control
- tools that save time

That's the bridge to metallurgy.
stone_chapter_intro.notes: Gated by survival_I_complete (chapterComplete); auto-completes when chapter is opened.

stone_marker_formalize.summary:
### Formalize the marker (Camp Utility I)

Reinforce the marker spot with a small kit: rope, bundled branches, stones.

Not magic -- you're just establishing "this is where tools and materials live."
That one habit saves more time than any single tool.

[hint]**Branch bundle**: gather sturdy branches or forest twigs, then bind them with rope or plant fiber. Hover `branch_bundle` in your inventory and press **R** to see the exact recipe.[/hint]
stone_marker_formalize.loreShort: A named spot with routine tools is the original workshop.
stone_marker_formalize.lore:
### What this is (historically)

Not an upgrade -- just a fixed place where tools and habits live:
- a known working surface
- ties always on hand
- materials kept where you use them

[hint]Quest consumes the kit to represent building/reinforcing the station at camp.[/hint]

tasks.desc: Have or place your camp marker at the work spot.
Pay the Camp Utility I kit (reinforcement materials).

stone_fuel_discipline.summary:
### Fuel discipline (dry buffer)

You will need dry wood constantly. Get ahead of it now.

Build a rack, keep it stocked. Rainy days don't get to delete your progress if you have a buffer.
stone_fuel_discipline.loreShort: Wet wood is a bad time. A dry rack is a good time.
stone_fuel_discipline.lore:
### The point

You are not hoarding. You are buying reliability.

[hint]A buffer also lets you focus on building things instead of constantly re-gathering fuel.[/hint]

tasks.desc: Have or place a firewood rack in camp.
Stock a basic dry buffer (small logs, medium logs, kindling).

stone_charcoal_clamp.summary:
### Charcoal clamp (predictable heat)

Charcoal is wood transformed into portable, reliable heat -- it burns hotter and steadier than raw wood.

Build an earth-mound clamp and run a first batch.
This isn't smelting fuel yet -- it's just "better heat for the kiln."

Load it. Start it. Walk away. It doesn't need you standing there.
stone_charcoal_clamp.loreShort: Charcoal is the bridge between 'campfire' and 'actually firing ceramics properly.'
stone_charcoal_clamp.lore:
### Why charcoal first

You can fire ceramics more reliably with charcoal than raw wood -- hotter, steadier, more controllable.

Making charcoal takes time and a lot of wood. That's intentional. It keeps things moving at a realistic pace.

tasks.desc: Have or place a charcoal clamp / earth-mound kiln.
Produce a first batch of charcoal.

stone_clay_work.summary:
### Clay work (greenware)

Gather workable clay and shape some pots.

Unfired clay is fragile -- it's called greenware, and yes, it will break if it gets wet.
This step is about **forming**, not firing. The kiln comes next.

[hint]**Where to get clay:** at the camp marker, craft `e1_wash_dirt_for_clay` -- 4× `dirt` + 1× `bark_bucket_water` + 1× `plant_fibers` -> 1× `clay`. The dirt is mined from any grass/soil terrain; the plant fibers act as a sieve to settle the heavy clay fraction out of the dirt slurry. Slow and lossy on purpose -- real clay-from-soil yields ~25%. Hover `clay` and press **R** to see the recipe.[/hint]

[hint]Hover `clay_pot` and press **R** to see the shaping recipe (4 clay -> 1 pot at the camp marker).[/hint]
stone_clay_work.loreShort: Pottery begins as shaped mud. Firing is what makes it real.
stone_clay_work.lore:
### Honest early ceramics

The earliest pots were hand-coiled, sun-dried, and fragile.
People used them anyway -- even a leaky clay bowl beats carrying water in cupped hands.

Firing came later, once someone noticed that pots left near a fire got harder.

The dirt-wash method (elutriation) is how clay was actually separated from soil for thousands of years before commercial clay deposits were exploited. The water + fiber sieve is older than agriculture.

tasks.desc: Collect workable clay.
Hand-shape clay pots (greenware -- unfired).

stone_kiln_fire.summary:
### Fire clay into ceramics

Build a simple earthen kiln and run your first firing.

Unfired clay is mud with ambition. Fired clay is actually durable. This is the difference.

Load it. Start it. Walk away. It doesn't need you standing there.
stone_kiln_fire.loreShort: Kilns turn fragile greenware into containers that hold water and survive the rain.
stone_kiln_fire.lore:
### What firing changes

Unfired clay hates water and cracks from impacts.
Fired clay survives both.

Once ceramics exist, storage and cooking stop being constant emergencies.

tasks.desc: Have or place a simple earthen kiln.
Fire a first run: bricks and vessels.

stone_containers_online.summary:
### Containers online

A container at camp means one fewer trip per day. Stack enough and suddenly you're not running back for water every hour.

Keep a water bucket at camp and some fired vessels for storage and carry.
stone_containers_online.loreShort: The bark bucket and clay vessel are quiet heroes of camp life.
stone_containers_online.lore:
### What changes

When you can carry and store reliably:
- you stop re-fetching basics constantly
- you can buffer food and water
- your hands are free to actually do work

tasks.desc: Have a water bucket available in camp.
Keep a full bucket of water at camp.
Have fired vessels ready for storage and carry.

stone_dry_cache.summary:
### Dry storage (pit cache)

Grain you gathered last week can still feed you today -- if you stored it right.

Get a starter batch and put it somewhere covered and dry.
stone_dry_cache.loreShort: A pit cache is basically a prehistoric pantry. Except underground. And without shelves.
stone_dry_cache.lore:
### Reality

Grain and seeds store well when kept dry and away from moisture.
The cache is the invention: it makes time and distance cheaper.

tasks.desc: Gather a starter batch of grain or seeds.
Have or place a covered pit cache for dry storage.

stone_quern_grind.summary:
### Grinding (saddle quern)

Stored grain is potential. Ground grain is food you can actually cook with.

Get a saddle quern and grind a starter batch of meal and flour.
It's one of the oldest labor tools in human history -- probably for good reason.
stone_quern_grind.loreShort: Grinding converts stored grain into usable ingredients. Your arms will feel it.
stone_quern_grind.lore:
### Why this matters

Meal for porridge and simple breads. Flour for better recipes later.

The quern is a genuine bottleneck early on -- which is the point.

tasks.desc: Have a saddle quern available (crafted or obtained).
Grind a starter batch of meal and flour.

stone_food_buffer.summary:
### Food buffer (tomorrow isn't luck)

Put food somewhere on purpose and leave it there.

A bad day shouldn't erase a week of progress. Stock enough to cover a couple rough days.
stone_food_buffer.loreShort: Buffers are how camps stop collapsing every time something goes wrong.
stone_food_buffer.lore:
### Buffer definition

Not endless stockpiles -- just enough that a single bad day doesn't delete progress.

[hint]This is also where spoilage mechanics can come into play later.[/hint]

tasks.desc: Have or place a food storage pit in camp.
Have a small buffer of safe foods ready.
Cook a couple simple rations at the hearth.

stone_camp_zones.summary:
### Camp zones + sanitation

Keep sparks away from bedding. Keep waste away from everything. Keep food storage separate from both.

That's it. That's the whole quest. It's boring, it's historically accurate, and it genuinely matters.
stone_camp_zones.loreShort: Fire, food, and filth don't mix. The original camp rule.
stone_camp_zones.lore:
### Why this is historically honest

People learn quickly:
- don't sleep next to sparks
- don't store food next to waste
- don't let the whole camp become a trash smear

A midden is primitive sanitation -- unglamorous, but real.

tasks.desc: Have or place food storage, a waste midden, and a fuel rack as separate camp zones.

stone_stone_toolkit.summary:
### Basic stone toolkit

Build the set of tools you'll reach for every day:

- **Hammerstone** -- strikes, cracks, and flakes
- **Stone flake** -- cuts and scrapes
- **Hafted knife** -- more control than a bare flake, safer too
- **Ground axe** -- actual wood processing without suffering

You've been improvising. Stop that.

[hint]**Two stone types, two paths:** smooth `stone_river_rounded` drops from rocks (and is the preferred form for knapping flakes). Rougher generic `stone` also drops from rocks, OR you can hand-cleave a river stone into chunks (`t0_hand_cleave_stone`, 1:1). The ground axe and quern saddle recipes ask for plain `stone`; the hammerstone and knife flake recipes use `stone_river_rounded`. Hover each tool and press R to see which form its recipe wants.[/hint]
stone_stone_toolkit.loreShort: A proper toolkit is the difference between 'it sort of works' and 'it works.'
stone_stone_toolkit.lore:
### Tool roles

- Hammerstone: percussion, shaping, cracking
- Flake: cutting and scraping
- Hafted blade: stronger, controllable, safer grip
- Ground axe: wood processing at a reasonable pace

The "river-rounded vs cleaved chunk" distinction mirrors real stone-age toolmaking: tumbled cobbles knap predictably, rougher fragments are better stock for grinding because they hold a flat face when worked.

tasks.desc: Have the full basic stone toolkit available (crafted or obtained).

stone_checkpoint.summary:
### Tier 1 complete -- Stone camp online

Your camp actually runs now:

- marker confirmed and reinforced
- controlled hearth, sorted fuel
- dry fuel buffer stocked
- charcoal production running
- kiln firing ceramics
- water carried, vessels for storage
- grain ground and buffered
- food stored and a buffer ahead of you
- zones laid out, sanitation handled
- stone toolkit complete

**Next:** Tier 2 can ask for real metallurgy and heavy heat work without your camp falling apart while you're busy.
stone_checkpoint.loreShort: From 'surviving' to 'maintaining a functional camp.' Those are genuinely different things.
stone_checkpoint.lore:
### The line this draws

From here forward, the game can assume you have:
- buffers (fuel, food, water)
- containers
- controlled heat
- basic grain processing
- a readable camp layout
- tools that save labor

That's enough stability to start real industry.

stone_first_farm.summary:
### First Furrow

You have seeds from the survival chapter. Now actually use them.

The hoe is in your stone toolkit. Equip it and swing at any grass or dirt terrain to till a patch.
Walk up to the tilled soil with seeds in your inventory and press **F** to plant.

Crops grow over time. Come back when the color changes to bright -- press **F** to harvest.
You get the crop plus a seed back, so the loop sustains itself.

**Recommended start:** plant turnips first (fastest growth), then carrot once you have the hang of it.

[hint]Crops will be eaten by nearby animals if there's no scarecrow. Craft one from 2 forest twigs + 2 bark strips at the hand grid and place it within ~20 studs.[/hint]
[hint]Farm plots are excluded from the erosion system -- tilled soil won't revert while seeds are planted.[/hint]
stone_first_farm.loreShort: Tilling and planting closes the survival food loop and gives you a renewable calorie supply that doesn't require wandering.
stone_first_farm.lore:
### Why farm?

Hunting and foraging work, but they're unreliable and distance-dependent.
A farm works while you're doing other things.

Two rows of turnips takes less time per calorie than any hunting route, doesn't require knowing biomes, and produces seeds so the supply is self-sustaining from the first harvest.

Once farming is running, food becomes a background resource rather than a daily emergency.

tasks.desc: Have seeds ready (carrot or turnip -- sort seed mix at the camp marker if needed).
Harvest your first crop.
Optional: also harvest turnips for a mixed food buffer.

stone_butchering_loop.summary:
### Meat on the Rack

Foraging covers calories. Hunting covers protein, hide, and tools.

Set up a **butchering block** and a **drying rack**. These two stations close the hunting loop:
kill an animal → butcher for carcass → drying rack for rawhide cord, dried meat, or hides.

**How to hunt:**
Equip a stone flake (or any tool) from your hotbar. Walk up to a passive animal and left-click to strike. Collect the carcass with F.

**At the butchering block (press F with carcass):**
- Meat → cook at campfire for food
- Hide → drying rack for rawhide cord (strong lashing; better than rope for some items)
- Bones → tools, meal, or decoration

**At the drying rack:**
- Raw hide → rawhide cord (drops in a few in-game hours)
- Raw meat → dried meat (preserves without a campfire)
stone_butchering_loop.loreShort: Butchering and drying close the hunting loop: carcass → meat + hide + bones, hide → rawhide cord.
stone_butchering_loop.lore:
### Why rawhide?

Rawhide cord is stronger than twisted plant fiber rope for some applications.
It's also how the bedroll_basic (the better bedroll) gets made, and how the iron-age
wire-drawing bench gets lashed together later.

Every hide you dry is a tool component you don't have to go looking for later.

tasks.desc: Have or place a butchering block and a drying rack.
Have some rawhide cord (butcher an animal, put the hide on the drying rack).
Optional: also have some raw venison ready to cook.

stone_token_convert.title: Trade In: Survival → Industry
stone_token_convert.summary: Exchange Tier I survival tokens for Tier II industry tokens. Repeatable.

nodes.title: Get a Starter Pile + Mark a Spot
Set Up a Night That Doesn't Wipe You

t0_join_intro.summary:
### Conduvia

You woke up here with nothing. Happens to the best of us.

Stone, sticks, and the next few hours -- that's the starting inventory.
The long-term plan is to build systems that build systems, but first: survive tonight.

**Four things will get you if you ignore them:**

1. **Water**
2. **Fire**
3. **Food**
4. **Sleep**

In that order, roughly. Try not to lose track of the first one while solving the fourth.

**The rungs:** Tier 0 (stone) → Tier 1 (camp) → Tier 2 (steam + steel) → Tier 3+ (electric + automation). The bottom is slow on purpose.

[hint]Open Settings → Controls to rebind anything.[/hint]
t0_join_intro.loreShort: Water → fire → food → sleep. Stone, then camp, then steam.
t0_join_intro.lore:
### Controls

| Action | Key |
|---|---|
| Quest Log | **`** (backtick) |
| Inventory | **E** |
| Interact / Pick up | **F** |
| Menu / Back | **Backspace** |
| Recipes for item | **R** (hover item) |
| Uses for item | **U** (hover item) |
| Drop one | **Q** |
| Map | **M** |
| Sprint | **Left Shift** |
| Crouch | **Left Ctrl** |

**Backspace will almost always go back or close a panel.**

### How to gather things

Most materials come from walking up to something and pressing **F**.

- **Plant fibers** -- F on tall grass, ferns, or leafy shrubs
- **River stones** -- F on rocks near water or rocky ground
- **Bark slabs / bark strips** -- F on tree trunks (aim at the bark, not the canopy)
- **Twigs / sticks** -- F on fallen branches and forest floor debris
- **Berries / nuts** -- F on shrubs and tree drip lines
- **Dry logs** -- F on dead snags (the bare-trunk dead trees)

If nothing happens when you press F: try crouching (**Left Ctrl**) or getting closer.

[hint]Hover anything in your inventory and press **U** to see every recipe that uses it. R goes the other way -- "what recipes make this?"[/hint]

### Quest book

The left rail is the chapter list -- each chapter is one tier of progression. Nodes inside a chapter are quests; lines between them are prerequisites. Click any node to see what it needs and what it rewards.

Bookmarks (left rail of the crafting pane) let you pin favorite items and recipes you're actively tracking.

### Crafting + items

Press **R** on any item to see recipes that produce it. Press **U** to see what it's used in. Press **A** to favorite it. Some recipes need a station -- the quest will say which one. Drop a station from your inventory to place it.

### Death + respawn

Dying leaves a **gravestone** at the death site holding your inventory. A waypoint auto-marks it on the map so you can walk back. The grave doesn't expire and is open to anyone nearby.

Craft a **bedroll** and hold **F** on it to claim a spawn point. You'll respawn there instead of the default pad.

### What kind of game is this?

Conduvia is a survival → automation → industry game. The loop: gather → craft → build → automate → repeat, at larger scale each time. The challenge isn't combat -- it's managing complexity without letting things fall apart.
t0_join_intro.notes: Shown once on first join; auto-completes after delay. Folds in former t0_chapter_intro + t0_controls_intro (2026-05-28) and the Welcome chapter (2026-06-02).

t0_make_cordage.summary:
### Cordage

Rope is the duct tape of prehistory. It shows up in basically every recipe that isn't just "rock."

Get some. More than you think you need.

[hint]**Plant fibers come from three places:** (1) **fern shrubs** drop them directly when you press F, (2) **willow shrubs** at riverside drop them, OR (3) if neither grows nearby, **ret bark**: pick `forest_bark_strip` (F on a tree trunk -- aim at the bark, not the canopy), put 3 strips + 1 `bark_bucket_water` in the camp marker and craft `plant_fibers`. The water soaks the bast fiber loose from the woody cortex; the bucket comes back empty.[/hint]

[hint]Rope itself is hand-crafted from 3 plant_fibers. R on `rope` in your inventory shows it.[/hint]
t0_make_cordage.loreShort: Binding things together is the original force multiplier.
t0_make_cordage.lore:
### Cordage isn't flashy -- it just keeps showing up.

The three obtain paths for plant fibers (fern, willow, bark-retting) mean you're never truly stuck for cordage as long as you have a forest to work with.

tasks.desc: Have at least 2 rope in your inventory.
Craft rope from plant fibers.

t0_scavenge_basics.summary:
### Get a starter pile + mark a spot

Grab a bit of everything, then pick somewhere to work from.

The temptation early on is to do "just one more trip." Resist it. Grab a bunch now while you're already out.
t0_scavenge_basics.loreShort: A marked spot turns aimless wandering into an actual work loop.
t0_scavenge_basics.lore:
### Batches, not crumbs

Every trip should bring back more than you need right now.
Early survival falls apart when every task becomes a separate emergency.

tasks.desc: Gather a basic starter pile: twigs, stone, fibers.
Twist a quick rope tie (you'll use cordage constantly).
Craft a simple camp marker.
Place your camp marker somewhere sensible. Not in water. Not on a cliff. (You could, I guess.)

t0_make_sharp_edge.summary:
### Get a cutting edge

Your hands are terrible scissors. Fix that.

A stone flake is the first tool that earns its name -- something that actually cuts on purpose.
t0_make_sharp_edge.loreShort: One real cutting edge saves an hour of terrible improvising.
t0_make_sharp_edge.lore:
### Real tech

A flake is the sharp piece you get when you strike stone against stone the right way.
Knapping is a genuine skill. In-game you just craft it -- appreciate the shortcut.

### Hunting basics

A stone flake is also your first hunting tool. To kill an animal:

1. **Equip** the stone flake (or any tool) from your hotbar.
2. Walk up to an animal and **left-click** to strike.
3. Keep swinging -- most animals take several hits. Predators (wolves, bears) fight back.

When an animal dies it leaves a **carcass** on the ground. Press **F** to pick it up.
Take it to a **butchering block** (basic) and press F to break it down into meat, hide, and bones.

**Start with passive animals:** Rabbit, Bird, Deer, Cow, or Sheep won't attack you.
Wolves and bears will. Start passive and upgrade your tools before picking fights with predators.

[hint]The hide you get from butchering is how you make `rawhide_cord` -- essential for the trapping quest and better tools.[/hint]

tasks.desc: Have a sharp stone flake.
Craft a basic stone flake.

t0_find_water.summary:
### Solve water (for now)

Find a water source, bring it back, make it safe.

The real problem isn't finding water -- it's the ten round trips after your camp is set up somewhere else.
Make a bark bucket. Carrying water in cupped hands is technically possible but deeply inefficient.
t0_find_water.loreShort: Distance is annoying. A bucket makes it less annoying.
t0_find_water.lore:
### Carrying water is a container problem

Early camps don't fail because there's no water.
They fail because water is a long walk, five times a day.

### Boiling guidance

- Rolling boil for **1 minute** at low altitude.
- Rolling boil for **3 minutes** at high altitude.

If your container can't sit in flame (like a bark bucket), use hot-stone boiling.

tasks.desc: Gather bark strips and a dry stick (for the bucket handle).
Craft a bark bucket (3 bark strips + 2 plant fibers + 1 stick, at camp marker).
Bring water back to your camp.
Treat the water so it's safe to drink.

t0_light_fire.summary:
### Make fire repeatable

Getting fire isn't the hard part. Keeping it alive is.

Three-layer stack -- each one depends on the last:
- **Tinder** -- catches the spark (dry fiber tinder)
- **Kindling** -- builds it into a flame (dry kindling sticks)
- **Fuel** -- keeps it going long enough to matter (small dry sticks or logs)

To **light** the campfire: equip the **fire_drill_kit**, walk up to a placed campfire, and **hold RMB**. An animation plays; after a moment the fire catches. The kit wears with use -- craft a spare.

[hint]The fire ring (6 river stones at camp marker) is optional -- a campfire without a ring still works, it just doesn't look intentional.[/hint]
t0_light_fire.loreShort: Fire is a system, not a lucky strike.
t0_light_fire.lore:
### Controlled fire

You want: small fire, steady feed, easy to kill.
If you don't have enough fuel, cooking and boil timers pause -- and that is annoying.

**The fire_drill_kit** is a usable tool: equip it, hold RMB near the campfire. It spins, uses tinder, and the fire catches.

tasks.desc: Have a fire drill kit in your inventory.
Place a survival campfire (crafted at the camp marker).
Optional: build a stone fire ring first (6 river stones at camp marker).
Optional: stock tinder and kindling before building.
Optional: stock dry fuel sticks.
Craft a basic fire drill kit (camp marker: 2 dry sticks + rope + stone flake).
Build a stone fire ring (camp marker: 6 river stones).
Build a survival campfire (camp marker: fire ring + tinder + kindling + logs).

t0_first_meal.summary:
### Get some calories in

Go find food. Cook some of it.

Different shrubs and trees drop different fruits and nuts -- blueberry, blackberry, hawthorn, sea buckthorn, juniper, rowan; hazelnut, acorn, nut bundles. Any pair of the **same** kind sorts at hand into one safe-edible portion (R-key the berry to see the recipe). Cooked food beats raw food.
t0_first_meal.loreShort: A full belly makes everything less terrible.
t0_first_meal.lore:
### Don't eat it all immediately

If you're always running at zero food, every other task gets harder.

Cook a small batch, eat one, keep the rest. Running a surplus sounds boring -- it isn't, once you see what it unlocks.

[hint]The world has an **assortment** of fruits and nuts, not just one generic kind. Pick whichever your area happens to grow -- they all funnel into the same `food_berries_safe` / `food_nuts_safe` output.[/hint]

tasks.desc: Gather a small amount of safe foods.
Have at least one simple cooked ration ready.
Cook one simple ration at your campfire.

t0_sleep_setup.summary:
### Set up a night that doesn't wreck you

The ground steals your body heat. Put something between you and it.

Make a straw bedroll, find somewhere reasonably sheltered, and put it down.

[hint]**To sleep:** place the bedroll, walk up to it, and press **E** to interact.
You'll get a rest prompt. Sleeping restores stamina and skips to morning.[/hint]

[hint]Hover `bedroll_straw` and press **R** to see the crafting recipe
(2× thatch bundle + plant fibres, at a camp marker).[/hint]
t0_sleep_setup.loreShort: Sleeping on bare ground isn't brave -- it's just cold.
t0_sleep_setup.lore:
### Bedding is layered

Grass, leaves, dry boughs -- piled thick.
If the ground's cold, you'll feel it through almost anything thin.

Wind and wet can drop your temperature even above freezing.
A windbreak and a roof matter as much as the bedding itself.

tasks.desc: Have or place a straw bedroll.
Craft a straw bedroll (2× thatch bundle + plant fibres, at camp marker).
Place your bedroll somewhere sheltered, then press E to sleep.

t0_checkpoint.summary:
### Tier 0 -- Stable

Not bad for someone who started with nothing.

You can now:

- get water without a trip every hour
- make fire on purpose
- keep food coming
- sleep without bleeding heat all night

**Reward: First Camp Gift** -- a starter kit to get Tier 1 moving.
Includes a fire ring, bedroll, storage pits, waste midden, firewood rack, water level gauge, and rope.
Nothing duplicates what you already have.

Next: turn that marked spot into an actual camp.
t0_checkpoint.loreShort: From 'trying not to die' to 'actually functional.' Good start.
t0_checkpoint.lore:
### This is the line

The next chapter (Tier 1) assumes you can stay alive while you build things.

t0_fishing.summary:
### First Cast

Still water holds food.

Craft a basic fishing rod at your camp marker and try your luck near a river or pond.
It takes patience -- the float bobs, then dips. Press **F** when it dips.

**How it works:**

1. Craft a **fishing rod** at the camp marker (2 dry sticks + 3 plant fibers + 1 stone knife flake).
2. Stand within 14 studs of water. Right-click to cast.
3. Wait for the float to dip -- then press **F** to reel in.

[hint]The bite window is short (~1.8 s). Watch the timing bar and don't wait too long.[/hint]
[hint]Missed the window? Right-click to cast again. Rod has limited durability -- around 20 catches.[/hint]
t0_fishing.loreShort: Still water holds food if you have the patience to wait for it.
t0_fishing.lore:
### Fishing is patience, not luck.

The float dips when the fish strikes. React within the window or the fish spits the hook.

A good riverside camp near the water means you can fish between other tasks without burning much time.

tasks.desc: Craft a basic fishing rod.
Catch a raw fish.

t0_farming_intro.summary:
### Dirt Under Your Nails

Foraging works today. Next week it won't.

Gathering seeds now means you hit Tier 0.5 with something ready to plant.

**Getting seeds:**

Flowers and spent grass heads drop `forest_seed_mix_dry` when you press F. Take 3 handfuls to your **camp marker** and sort them into crop seeds (recipe: "sort seed mix" in the camp marker's crafting list). This gives flax, turnip, AND carrot seeds in one pass.

**The full farming loop (needs hoe, available in Tier 0.5):**

1. Equip a hoe and swing it at open ground (grass or dirt) to till a patch.
2. Walk up to the tilled soil with seeds in your inventory and press **F** to plant.
3. Wait for the sprout to change colour -- it's ripe when it glows bright.
4. Press **F** to harvest: you get crop + a seed back, so the supply sustains itself.

[hint]Turnip is the staple starter crop -- fast and hardy. Carrot is a bit slower but useful for variety.[/hint]
t0_farming_intro.loreShort: Gather seeds now. The hoe and the soil wait in Tier 0.5.
t0_farming_intro.lore:
### Why bother with farming?

Hunting and foraging are unreliable and biome-dependent. Farming gives you a controlled, renewable food loop that works regardless of how far you've explored.

One tilled row of turnips takes less time per calorie than any hunting route.

Starting the seed collection now means you hit Tier 0.5 with something to plant immediately rather than having to backtrack for seeds after you've built the hoe.

tasks.desc: Have carrot seeds. Sort them at your camp marker (3 seed mix → all three seed types) or via the hand grid (2 seed mix + plant fibers → carrot seed).
Also have some turnip seeds for the staple crop.

t0_animal_trapping.summary:
### A Quieter Hunt

Not everything is worth killing.

A lasso and a basket let you move small animals around -- relocate prey closer to camp, or just keep a few around for the mid-game when you need steady yields without burning through your hunting stock.

**How it works:**

1. Craft a **rope lasso** (2 rope + 1 rawhide cord) at the hand-crafting grid.
2. Craft a **wicker basket** (2 willow + 1 plant fibers).
3. Equip the lasso. Walk within about 15 studs of a Rabbit, Bird, or Owl and left-click.
4. The animal is packed into a basket in your inventory.
5. Equip the occupied basket and left-click to release the animal wherever you want it.

**Getting rawhide cord:**
To craft the lasso you need `rawhide_cord`. Kill a passive animal (Rabbit, Deer, Cow) by equipping your stone flake and left-clicking it. Pick up the carcass with F, take it to a **butchering block**, and press F to get hide. Then hand-craft: `hide + stone_flake → rawhide_cord`.

**Alternative -- snares:**
Rather than chasing animals, set a passive snare. Craft a `snare_simple` (rope + 2 forest_twigs_fine, hand grid) and drop it near an animal trail. Small game will walk into it and you'll find a carcass when you come back.

[hint]**Rope lasso** is single-use -- it's consumed on a successful catch. Bring a few if you plan to move a group.[/hint]
[hint]For **medium animals** (Cow, Sheep, Deer, Red Fox) you need a **wooden cage** (2 wood + 1 rope + 1 rawhide cord). Same mechanic.[/hint]
[hint]**Wolves** and **bears** can't be lassooed. Craft a **wolf lure** (cooked meat + rope) or **bear lure** (2 safe berries + rope) instead -- place it and step back. The animal arrives on its own, then you deal with it however you like.[/hint]
t0_animal_trapping.loreShort: Move animals without a fight. Useful for farming setups later.
t0_animal_trapping.lore:
### Why bother?

Lasso-and-basket is a soft version of ranching. You're not taming the animals -- they're still wild when you release them. But being able to relocate them means you can consolidate livestock near camp, seed a pond with birds, or just move a cow somewhere convenient before the mid-game farms come online.

The wooden cage opens up deer relocation, which feeds into leather supply chains before you get consistent hunting going.

### Snare vs lasso

Snares are for food. Lassos are for relocation. Both require rawhide cord, but snares are lighter to set up and let you trap passively while you do other things. A few snares set before bed means meat in the morning without a hunt.

tasks.desc: Craft a rope lasso.
Craft a wicker basket (2 willow wands + 1 plant fibers, hand-crafted).
Catch a small animal (have any occupied basket).
Optional: also set a snare for passive trapping (rope + 2 twigs, hand grid).

t0_first_storage.summary:
### Keep What You Find

Your pack fills fast. A bark storage crate holds 27 stacks and keeps its contents between sessions -- everything you place in it stays there when you log off.

**To craft one**, open your camp marker and queue a bark storage crate:
- 8 × forest bark strip
- 2 × branch bundle
- 2 × rope

The camp marker shows a progress bar while it builds (~50 s). When done, place the crate near your bedroll.

**To use it**, left-click the crate to open it. Shift+click moves items between your inventory and the crate instantly.

tasks.title: Craft a bark storage crate
tasks.desc: Queue 'bark storage crate' at the camp marker (8 bark strips + 2 branch bundles + 2 rope).

tasks.title: Place your storage crate
tasks.desc: Equip the bark storage crate and press F to place it near camp.
